coredata.dc:title: Nanozyme-based cancer theranostics: A scientometric analysis and comprehensive review

coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes, possessing enzyme-like characteristics, have emerged as valuable nanomaterials in biomedical applications owing to their advantages over natural enzymes. Since nanozymes exhibit several vital and excellent catalysis activities to mediate multiple oxidation-reduction reactions that can further induce changes of cancer cells and tumor microenvironment, their applications in cancer theranostics have attracted widespread attention. Thus, this scientometric analysis aims to offer a comprehensive overview of the advancements and burgeoning trends in this field. The comprehensive assessment of scientometric analysis encompasses focal areas of inquiry, emergent research paradigms, and bibliometric profiles. The study analyzes the annual distribution of publications and citations, the geographical distribution across countries and institutions, categorizations by subject matter, contributions from academic journals, authorship patterns, and cited references. Drawing upon the findings of our scientometric evaluation coupled with an integrative review of pertinent contemporary literature, comprehensive analyses of this domain have been executed. First, the classification of nanozymes used in cancer diagnosis and treatment is reviewed. Succeeding that, various nanozyme-based approaches for both in vitro and in vivo cancer detection are outlined, supplemented by relevant examples. Subsequently, distinct nanozyme-based treatment strategies are delineated, including catalytic activity-based strategies, such as ROS generation, GSH depletion, starvation therapy, oxygen supply, and TME remodeling and immunogenic cell death, and nanostructure-based non-catalytic strategies, such as drug delivery, photodynamic and sonodynamic effects, photothermal and magnetic hyperthermia effects, prodrug activation, and ion imbalance. Finally, seven prevalent treatment modalities (catalytic therapy combined with chemodynamic therapy, immunotherapy, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, photothermal therapy or magnetic hyperthermia therapy, photodynamic therapy, and sonodynamic therapy) are categorized and elaborated on. In conclusion, this comprehensive review aims to inspire innovative research endeavors in related domains and accelerate progress in nanozyme-mediated cancer diagnostics and therapeutics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The annual distribution of publications related to cancer theranostics based on nanozymes. (A) The annual distribution of publications on the entire topic. The annual distribution of publications in the fields of (B) cancer diagnosis, (C) cancer treatment, and (D) cancer theranostics, respectively. The relevant publications records of year 2024 was incorporated from WoSCC by the end of May 13, 2024.
The global distribution of publications in this domain. The publication number range is from 1 to 1273. The shade of the color reflects the quantity of publications.
Co-citation network of seminal references. The time range is from 2009 to 2023. Literature is presented by the name of the first author and the year the publication was published.

Cluster map and temporal distribution analyses of keywords. (A) A Citespace-generated map of keyword clusters, with a modularity Q-value of 0.7238, a weighted mean silhouette S-value of 0.8756, and a harmonic mean (Q, S) of 0.7925. (B) Temporal distribution of keywords specific to the field.
Timeline view of keywords, informed by clustering analysis. The different colors of nodes represent the year in which they first appeared, and the cluster names all have different colors.
The 35 keywords demonstrating the most robust citation bursts within the field. The red bold lines represent the time range when keywords are bursting a lot.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of nanozyme-based cancer detections.

ce:figure.ce:source: (A) Nanozyme-based in vitro cancer detections include (i) colorimetric analysis (reprint permission from [126]), (ii) photothermal analysis (reprint permission from [154]), (iii) electrochemical analysis (reprint permission from [155]), (iv) photoelectrochemical analysis (reprint permission from [70]), (v) fluorescence analysis (reprint permission from [156]), and (vi) electrochemiluminescence analysis (reprint permission from [157]). (B) Nanozyme-based in vivo cancer detections include (i) PAI (reprint permission from [158]), (ii) PTI (reprint permission from [159]), (iii) MRI (reprint permission from [160]), (iv) CT (reprint permission from [161]), (v) FLI, (reprint permission from [162]) and (vi) PET (reprint permission from [163]).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CrisprZyme: preamplification-free detection of non-coding RNAs in cancer. (A) Target RNA and a gRNA-Cas13 complex were mixed to initiate reporter RNA cleavage. The mix was added to a plate pre-coated with anti-FAM, followed by nanozyme and substrate addition for color development. (B) A brief outline of the steps involved in sample analysis. (C) Upregulated circRNAs in Prostate Cancer Biopsies: Quantification of circ-AURKA in ACA and NEPC via CrisprZyme. Data points and statistics were represented by box and whisker plots, with significance assessed via the Mann-Whitney test. Reprinted with permission from [168].

A photothermal cytosensor designed for the detection of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in breast cancer (BC-CTCs). (A) The schematic representation of the portable photothermal cytosensor designed for the direct capture and quantification of CTCs. (B) Temperature variations observed under different experimental conditions. (C) The linear correlation between temperature change (ΔT) and the logarithm of MCF-7 cell count. (D) Infrared thermal images captured under various conditions: (a) Blank, (b) Ti3C2@Au@Pt-P2, (c) Ti3C2@Au@Pt-P3, (d) Combined Ti3C2@Au@Pt-P2 and Ti3C2@Au@Pt-P3. (E) Infrared thermal images of the cytosensor when exposed to varying quantities of MCF-7 cells. Reprinted with permission from [154].

The synthesis and in vitro and in vivo verifications of MSPN for cancer therapy. (A) Synthesis routes for ORM and MSPN, featuring chemical structures of PFODBT, ORM, and PSMA. (B) Cellular and Tumor Imaging via MSPN Oxidase Mimicry. (i) Ratiometric NIRF imaging schema tied to nanozyme activity for monitoring therapeutic efficacy. (ii) Time-dependent fluorescence profiles of 4T1 cells exposed to MSPN or SPN. (iii) Fluorescence intensity ratios calculated from (ii). (iv) Cellular inhibition metrics in 4T1 cells post MSPN or SPN exposure. (v) Correlation between cellular inhibition rates and fluorescence intensity ratios. (vi) 4T1 cell viability in response to varying MSPN concentrations. (vii-x) In vivo imaging and therapeutic outcomes in Balb/c mice with 4T1 tumors following MSPN or SPN administration. (C) In vivo MSPN Tracking via Ratiometric NIRF-PA Imaging. (i) Schematic of ratiometric NIRF-PA imaging for real-time monitoring of MSPN oxidase mimicry in cancer therapy. (ii, iii) Post-injection fluorescence and photoacoustic snapshots in mice. (iv, v) Calculated fluorescence and photoacoustic intensity ratios. (vi, vii) Tumor development and histopathological evaluations in mouse cohorts treated with different formulations, such as control (PBS), SPN, and MSPN. Reprinted with permission from [203].

In vivo Applications and Imaging of FTRNPs. (A) Schematic illustration of FTRNPs. (B) Multimodal Imaging of FTRNPs in vivo. (i) In vivo FL imaging. (ii) FL images of organs and tumors 24 hours after FTNPs/FTRNPs administration. (iii) Quantified FL results. (iv) Quantification of photoacoustic (PA) signals before and after FTNPs/FTRNPs administration. (C) In vivo photoacoustic microscopy enhanced (PAMe) images, highlighting tumors. (D) Synergistic CDT and PTT in vivo. (i) In vivo infrared thermal images of tumor-bearing mice treated with PBS or FTRNPs, with or without magnetic targeting, under NIR laser irradiation. (ii) Tumor photographs post-treatments, including PBS or FTRNPs, with or without NIR laser irradiation, and with or without magnetic targeting. Reprinted with permission from [162].

Schematic illustration of nanozyme-based cancer treatment strategies. Catalytic activity-based strategies include 1) ROS generation, 2) GSH depletion, 3) Starvation therapy, 4) Oxygen supply, and 5) TME remodeling and ICD. Nanostructure-based non-catalytic strategies include 1) Drug delivery, 2) Photodynamic and sonodynamic effects, 3) Photothermal and magnetic hyperthermia effects, 4) Prodrug activation, and 5) Ion imbalance.

Schematic illustration of nanozyme-based cancer treatment approaches, including (A) Catalytic therapy induced/plus immunotherapy (reprint permission from [249]), (B) Catalytic therapy induced/plus CDT (reprint permission from [338]), (C) Catalytic therapy plus chemotherapy (reprint permission from [339]), (D) Catalytic therapy plus radiotherapy (reprint permission from [340]), (E) Catalytic therapy plus hyperthermia therapy (reprint permission from [68]), (F) Catalytic therapy plus PDT (reprint permission from [341]), and (G) Catalytic therapy plus SDT (reprint permission from [61]).

Therapeutic mechanism and in vivo efficacy of Ir-N5 SA/Cer. (A) Schematic illustration elucidating the therapeutic mechanism of Ir-N5 SA/Cer. (B) In vivo therapeutic efficacy. (i) Outline of the in vivo therapeutic protocol through a schematic illustration. (ii, iii) Tumor volume curves for different treatment groups. (iv) Tumor weights for mice in the treatment groups. (v) Post-treatment tumor inhibition rates. (vi) Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and TUNEL staining images of tumor sections. (vii) Survival percentages for mice subjected to different treatments. (viii) Body weights of mice throughout the course of various treatments. (ix) H&E staining images of slides from major organs. Reprinted with permission from [139].

Illustration and verification of RhRu/Ti3C2Tx nanozymes in osteosarcoma treatment. (A) RhRu/Ti3C2Tx synergistic mechanism in osteosarcoma treatment. (B) Therapeutic Efficacy in Osteosarcoma Model with sub-components including (i) RhRu/Ti3C2Tx therapeutic regimen illustration, (ii) thermal infrared images under 808 nm laser, (iii) time-dependent body weight changes, (iv) 14-day post-treatment relative tumor volume changes, (v) excised tumors after 14 days, (vi-viii) histopathological analyses featuring H&E, TUNEL, and HIF-1α, (ix) Antigen Ki67 expression, (x) in situ body weight changes over time, (xi) in situ relative tumor volume changes over 14 days, (xii) in situ tumors after 14 days, and (xiii, xiv) in situ histopathological features with H&E and TUNEL. Reprinted with permission from [359].

Illustration and verification of CM@Mn nanozyme in cancer therapy. (A) Schematic for CM@Mn nanozyme synthesis and manganese-enhanced catalytic immunotherapy with PD-1 blockade. (B) In vivo efficacy against primary Tumor featuring. (i) 4T1 therapy outline. (ii) Tumor growth trajectories. (iii) Day-21 t-SNE plots for intratumoral immune cell presence. (iv) Day-21 resected tumor weight. (v) Day-21 resected tumor images. (vi) Day-21 levels of TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-6, IL-12p70. (vii) Kaplan-Meier survival curves. (viii) Day-21 Tem and Tcm flow cytometry in mouse spleens. Reprinted with permission from [370].

Efficacy validation of NMPN nanozymes in the treatment of orthotopic liver tumor. (A) Schematic of model and treatment plan. (B) Infrared images of post-treatment tumor response. (C) Quantitative analysis of tumor bioluminescence. (D) Liver photos on day 31, tumors marked by arrows. (E) TUNEL and DAPI stain patterns in tumors. (F, G) Western blot and quantification of γ-H2AX, XPA, XPF levels. (H) Body weight changes post-treatment. (I) Survival curves post-treatment. (J) Mechanism schematic: NMPN effects on Pt-DNA adducts, XPA/XPF recruitment, and NER pathway disruption. Reprinted with permission from [73].

Theranostic applications of P-RuCu nanozyme in augmenting the efficacy of cancer radiotherapy. (A) A schematic representation of the P-RuCu nanozyme, elucidating its synthetic process. (B) (i) confocal microscopy images depicting γ-H2AX and DAPI staining in MDA-MB-231 cells after treatment. (ii) cell survival curves with differing X-ray dosages. (iii) a quantitative evaluation of γ-H2AX foci densities. (iv) a temporal assessment of relative tumor volume alterations within the MDA-MB-231 mouse model. (v) photographic records of resected tumors at day 12; and (vi) corresponding tumor weight measurements. Reprinted with permission from [54].

Illustration and validation of phototheranostic nanozyme PtSn BNCs in cancer therapy. (A) Depiction of PtSn BNCs highlighting their phototheranostic potential and the synergy of photothermal-enhanced catalytic therapy. (B) In vitro and in vivo Efficacy. (i) Photothermal performance curves of Pt50Sn50-PEG BNCs at various concentrations when exposed to an 808 nm laser. (ii) Evaluation of the thermal stability of Pt50Sn50-PEG BNCs under 808 nm laser irradiation. (iii) Assessment of cytotoxicity in 4T1 cells under different treatment conditions. (iv) Quantification of LDH release from 4T1 cells following treatment. (v) Quantitative caspase-3 levels in 4T1 cells post-treatment. (vi) Photographs of dissected tumors on day 15 post-treatment. (vii) Relative tumor weights in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice post-treatment. (viii) Tumor volume growth curves for all groups post-treatment. (ix) Pre- and post-intratumoral injection of Pt50Sn50-PEG BNCs, with corresponding CT value line profiles. (x) Time-dependent PA images post-intravenous administration of Pt50Sn50-PEG BNCs. Reprinted with permission from [161].

Multimodal synergistic antitumor mechanism of CoPc-Mn/Ti3C2T x -PEG/AS1411. (A) Schematic of CoPc-Mn/Ti3C2T x -PEG/AS1411 preparation and in vivo antitumor mechanism (PTT, PDT, CDT). (B) In vitro Studies: (i) H2O2 production under 808 nm irradiation (10 min). (ii) ESR spectra under 808 nm NIR irradiation. (iii) O2 generation under various conditions. (iv) UV–vis absorption spectra of MB with CoPc-Mn/Ti3C2T x -PEG. (v) Time-dependent MB degradation. (vi) ESR spectra with DMPO. (vii) DPBF absorption under 808 nm irradiation. (viii) Absorbance change of DPBF at 417 nm. (ix) ESR spectra of trapped singlet oxygen (1O2). (C) In vivo Studies: (i) Time-dependent PA images post-injection of CoPc-Mn/Ti3C2T x -PEG/AS1411. (ii) PA signal intensity evolution. (iii) Tumor volume growth post-treatment. Reprinted with permission from [158].

Design and applications of UIO-66-Au nanozyme for antitumor catalytic therapy plus SDT. (A) Synthesis of UIO-66-Au nanoparticles for enhanced sonodynamic and nanozyme therapy. (B) In vitro Properties: (i) DPBF degradation under ultrasonic irradiation. (ii) UV–visible absorption spectra of TMB. (iii, iv) ESR spectra showing 1O2 and •OH levels. (v) Proposed mechanism for enhanced sonodynamic therapy. (vi) UV–visible absorption spectra of TMB. (vii) Lineweaver-Burk plot demonstrating H2O2 generation. (viii) Proposed mechanism for intensified nanozyme activities. (C) In vivo Investigations: (i) Body weight changes in tumor-bearing mice. (ii) Tumor volume changes in response to treatments. (iii) Relative viability of MCF7 cells exposed to UIO-66 and UIO-66-Au NPs with and without US irradiation Reprinted with permission from [462].

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozymes, possessing enzyme-like characteristics, have emerged as valuable nanomaterials in biomedical applications owing to their advantages over natural enzymes. Since nanozymes exhibit several vital and excellent catalysis activities to mediate multiple oxidation-reduction reactions that can further induce changes of cancer cells and tumor microenvironment, their applications in cancer theranostics have attracted widespread attention. Thus, this scientometric analysis aims to offer a comprehensive overview of the advancements and burgeoning trends in this field. The comprehensive assessment of scientometric analysis encompasses focal areas of inquiry, emergent research paradigms, and bibliometric profiles. The study analyzes the annual distribution of publications and citations, the geographical distribution across countries and institutions, categorizations by subject matter, contributions from academic journals, authorship patterns, and cited references. Drawing upon the findings of our scientometric evaluation coupled with an integrative review of pertinent contemporary literature, comprehensive analyses of this domain have been executed. First, the classification of nanozymes used in cancer diagnosis and treatment is reviewed. Succeeding that, various nanozyme-based approaches for both in vitro and in vivo cancer detection are outlined, supplemented by relevant examples. Subsequently, distinct nanozyme-based treatment strategies are delineated, including catalytic activity-based strategies, such as ROS generation, GSH depletion, starvation therapy, oxygen supply, and TME remodeling and immunogenic cell death, and nanostructure-based non-catalytic strategies, such as drug delivery, photodynamic and sonodynamic effects, photothermal and magnetic hyperthermia effects, prodrug activation, and ion imbalance. Finally, seven prevalent treatment modalities (catalytic therapy combined with chemodynamic therapy, immunotherapy, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, photothermal therapy or magnetic hyperthermia therapy, photodynamic therapy, and sonodynamic therapy) are categorized and elaborated on. In conclusion, this comprehensive review aims to inspire innovative research endeavors in related domains and accelerate progress in nanozyme-mediated cancer diagnostics and therapeutics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes are defined as nanomaterials with enzyme-like characteristics [1,2]. Compared with natural enzymes, nanozymes have many advantages including high stability particularly under adverse conditions, cost-effectiveness, and enhanced catalytic activities [3,4]. These advantages of nanozymes attract more and more scholars into this field. In 2007, Yan Group was the first to describe the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles (NPs) (Fe3O4), which set a precedent of nanozymes [5]. Till now, thousands of nanozymes with different nanostructures, catalytic activities, or applications have been identified. In the last five years, studies with nanozymes have increased exponentially. These nanozymes have been found to have extensive applications in the fields of environmental protection, biosensing, and nanomedicine, etc.

In 2018, Fan et al. pioneered the systematic study of nanozymes for tumor catalytic therapy, marking a significant milestone in the extensive and profound explorations of nanozymes within the realm of tumor theranostics [6]. Later in 2019, Shi group defined nanocatalytic medicine as "catalytic reaction-based disease diagnosis and therapy using biocompatible nanomaterials" [7]. Moreover, nanozymes used for cancer detection and treatment are becoming the focus in the field of nanocatalytic medicine. Cancer remains a formidable challenge, as characterized by difficulty in diagnosing, high mortality rates, and high cost of treatment [8,9]. For cancer diagnosis, there are both in vitro and in vivo detection approaches. However, most of them face the problem of either low sensitivity or poor accuracy. For early cancer diagnosis, detection with both high sensitivity and accuracy is greatly needed. In the past decade, nanozyme-based cancer detection approaches with high specificity and signal amplification have been developed well, which may serve as promising tools in an early and precise cancer diagnosis. For current cancer treatment, a series of therapeutic approaches, ranging from traditional surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy to various novel therapies including targeted therapy, immunotherapy, hyperthermia therapy, photodynamic therapy, sonodynamic therapy, and chemodynamic therapy have been proposed. However, some of these approaches are effective for certain types of cancers [9]. Currently, many cancers are awaiting effective treatments. At this time, the nanozyme-based catalytic therapies combined with other conventional approaches have shown promise in preclinical studies.

Although there have been some recent reviews on nanozymes for cancer treatment and/or diagnosis, a comprehensive summary and discussion for this area is still lacking [10,11]. Considering the fast-paced development of nanozymes in both cancer detection and treatment, we will summarize the latest landscape of this area with a critical analysis. Moreover, this constitutes the first scientometric evaluation in this domain, encompassing annual publication and citation metrics, geographical and institutional distributions, subject classifications, contributions from academic journals, authorship patterns, cited references, and keyword trends. Ultimately, the intent of this review is to furnish enhanced understanding of pivotal research focal points and emergent trends in the field of oncology.

The data extraction for comprehensive scientometric analyses was carried out on July 26, 2023, yielding 1496 records. And a schematic representation of the study's methodology was illustrated in Figure S1A. Since scientometric analysis reflects the overall research status and possible trends in a field, many scientometric analyses inevitably miss some of the latest literatures due to the lengthy review and revision process before publication. However, this does not compromise the overall validity of the analysis, as evidenced by the substantial body of published literature [12–16]. It is important to note that we have updated the most readily available annual publication data for relevant literature up to May 13, 2024.

CiteSpace (Version 6.2.R4 Advanced) functions as a multifaceted tool, facilitating both interactive and exploratory examinations in a specific scientific domain [17]. Employing a blend of quantitative and qualitative methodologies, we endeavored to elucidate the structural framework of knowledge while identifying research hotspots and trends [18]. CiteSpace enables users to interact with the visual depiction of a field of scientific knowledge via several methods. It offers the flexibility to alter the visual output of analytical findings by modifying different settings in the foundational layout algorithms. Within the framework of this scientometric assessment, a total of 1496 records were transferred from the WoS to CiteSpace, saved as plain text files. The configurations for CiteSpace were as followed:
1)
Time frames extended from January 2007 through December 2023, with each segment aligning with an individual calendar year;
2)
The inclusion criteria targeted the 50 most recurrently cited or featured elements for each temporal segment;
3)
A trio of reduction algorithms were utilized, specifically, pathfinder, slicing-based network pruning, and merged network pruning.

Within the scope of this study, CiteSpace was utilized to dissect research contributions by country and institution, as well as to analyze referenced works, cluster formations, and timeline and timezone views. Additionally, a burst analysis of keywords was conducted.

VOS Viewer (Version 1.6.19) serves as an alternative software tool designed explicitly for the construction and visualization of scientometric maps [19]. Distinct from other scientometric utilities, VOS Viewer places particular emphasis on the quality of graphical representation, thereby facilitating the interpretation of expansive visualization maps [19]. All 1496 records retrieved from the WoSCC were exported into VOS Viewer in the form of tab-delimited text files. Initially, we opted to "construct a map predicated on bibliographic data." Subsequently, we selected the option to "ingest data from bibliographic database files." Upon ingesting the pertinent files, a tailored scientometric evaluation can be executed. Within the framework of this investigation, VOS Viewer was employed to scrutinize the contributions of authors and academic journals in this particular scientific domain.

To directly understand the topics of the selected publications, we manually classified these publications into six groups including nanozymes for cancer treatment, nanozymes for cancer detection, nanozymes for cancer treatment and detection, non-nanozymes, nanozymes for non-cancer/tumor fields, and others (Figure S1B). The incorporation and classification process were performed by three independent authors.

Temporal distribution patterns for academic papers within the domain from 2009 to 2024 were detailed in Fig. 1A. The dataset comprised a sum of 1936 papers. Up to the year 2014, the annual publication volume concerning nanozymes in oncology was sporadic and yielded fewer than ten papers annually. A modest ascendancy in the rate of annual publications was observed from 2014 through 2017. Subsequent to 2017, annual publication rates exhibited a rapid and sustained upward trajectory.

To further understand how publications in different subareas (nanozymes for cancer treatment/cancer detection/cancer treatment and detection) evolve from 2009 to 2024, the annual distributions of publications associated with nanozymes in cancer diagnosis, cancer treatment, and cancer theranostics were counted (Fig. 1B-D). It was found that compared with nanozymes for cancer diagnosis (Fig. 1B), the publications in the fields of nanozymes for cancer treatment (Fig. 1C) developed much later. After 2017, research on nanozymes for cancer treatment started to develop faster than the earlier years. The publications related to nanozymes for cancer treatment have multiplied nearly 10 times over the past six years. Moreover, nanozymes for both cancer detection and cancer treatment (theranostic nanozymes) have also obtained a lot of attention in recent five years (Fig. 1D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Worldwide geographic dispersion of countries and academic establishments
ce:section.ce:para: In the field of nanozymes for cancer, there were 59 countries or regions with at least one relevant publication ( Fig. 2 , Table S1). Among all the countries, China had an overwhelmingly high publication numbers (n=1254, accounting for 83.82 %). The United States ranked second in terms of publication numbers, with a much fewer publications than China (n=92). The United States and China also had a characteristic with high betweenness centralities (The United States: 0.44; China: 0.30), indicating their high influences in this field [20]. The potential reason for the high influence of the United States with a much smaller number of publications in this field is that their studies are characteristic with higher citation numbers, according to the data on the WoSCC. However, further examinations of these high impact publications showed that most of them were finished by working with Chinese scholars, which proves again that the dominant role of China in this field. Nevertheless, this also indicates the international cooperation of research may promote the influence of the publication.

Within this research landscape, 426 institutions registered a presence with at least one publication. Of these, a subset of 30 institutions distinguished themselves with a publication count exceeding 20 papers, and an additional 56 institutions had a record of more than ten publications. This stratification of institutional contributions elucidates the distribution of scholarly activity across various research entities. Among all the institutions, Chinese Academy of Sciences ranked first (n=403), followed by Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry (n=78), University of Science & Technology of China (n=77), Zhengzhou University (n=59), and Nanjing University (n=54). Moreover, it was found that the top 20 institutions with the highest number of publications in the world were all Chinese institutions (Table S2 ). In terms of betweenness centralities, the Chinese Academy of Sciences (betweenness centrality (bc=0.28), National Center for Nanoscience & Technology-China (bc=0.27), Jilin University (bc=0.21), Soochow University (bc=0.17), Nanjing University (bc=0.13), and Zhengzhou University (bc=0.11) exhibited noteworthy centrality within the network, thereby signaling its instrumental role in advancing the development of this particular scientific domain.

CiteSpace was employed to scrutinize the subject classifications associated with publications in the field of nanozymes for oncological applications. A total of 49 subject categories were encompassed in this examination. Ten of these subject categories were represented in more than 100 publications, as detailed in Table S3. Predominant categories included "Nanoscience & Nanotechnology" (n=577), "Materials Science, Multidisciplinary" (n=487), "Chemistry, Multidisciplinary" (n=483), "Chemistry, Physical" (n=289), "Chemistry, Analytical" (n=279), "Physics, Applied" (n=223), and "Materials Science, Biomaterials" (n=214). Notably, the subject categories "Chemistry, Multidisciplinary" (bc=0.83), "Nanoscience & Nanotechnology" (bc=0.68), and "Chemistry, Physical" (bc=0.48) demonstrated the highest betweenness centrality values, underscoring their pivotal roles in shaping the research landscape of this domain.

VOS Viewer was utilized to examine the authorship patterns, citation impact, and co-citation networks within this field. The analysis revealed the participation of 7335 authors in at least one publication, 257 of whom had five or more papers. This subset was subjected to co-authorship network mapping, uncovering 35 authors with over ten papers in the field. Leading contributors included PP Yang (n=26) and XG Qu (n=20), succeeded by Y Chen, SL Gai, J Lin, JS Ren, KL Fan, and Y Zhang (Table S4). The co-authorship analysis elucidated key research consortia, as depicted in Figure S2A .

In terms of citation metrics, 17 authors exceeded 1000 citations. Notably, XG Qu (n=3527) and JS Ren (n=3524) topped the list. Additional high-impact authors encompassed Y Chen, JL Shi, J Lin, KL Fan, XY Yan, and LZ Gao. Ratios of citations to documents (RCDs) were computed for these heavily cited contributors, revealing 36 authors with RCDs exceeding 100. JL Shi was unparalleled in this metric with an RCD of 419.14, amassing 2934 citations from merely seven relevant papers.

Co-citation analysis, conducted via VOS Viewer, implicated 29734 authors with at least one co-citation. A distilled list of the top 200 co-cited authors was constructed (Table S5). LZ Gao emerged as the most co-cited author (n=576), followed by a cohort including Y Zhang, Y Liu, JJX Wu, YY Huang, and H Wei. The co-citation network (Figure S2B) clarified the interrelations between these authors. Distinct clusters, indicated by color variations, suggest thematic cohesiveness within each cluster. Frequent co-citations within a cluster imply a higher degree of research similarity. Active co-citation relationships, such as the network involving LZ Gao, Y Zhang, H Wei, KL Fan, JJX Wu, H Wang, and YJ Song, were also manifest.

Utilizing VOS Viewer, the publication, citation, and co-citation landscapes of journals active in this field were examined. From 2009–2023, a total of 226 journals were identified as publishing outlets for research in this domain. Of these, 63 journals met a minimum criterion of publishing five or more documents in the subject area. Subsequently, these journals were incorporated into a citation network analysis, which was visually represented in Figure S2C.

The journal "ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces" emerged as the primary platform for disseminating research in this field, with 83 publications, followed by "Biosensors & Bioelectronics" (n=59), "Journal of Materials Chemistry B" (n=58), and "Chemical Engineering Journal" (n=53) (Table S6). For journals with a high citation impact, "ACS Nano" (n=3862), "Advanced Materials" (n=3632), and "Angewandte Chemie-International Edition" (n=3133) constituted the top three venues (Table S6).

Co-citation analysis of journals also yielded compelling data (Figure S2D, Table S7). A total of 4035 journals were identified that had accrued at least one co-citation. Of these, 123 journals surpassed the threshold of 100 co-citations. Specifically, "Advanced Materials" (n=4626), "ACS Nano" (n=4610), and "Angewandte Chemie-International Edition" (n=4464) were the most co-cited journals. Additionally, journals like "Journal of the American Chemical Society" (n=3553), "ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces" (n=3353), "Analytical Chemistry" (n=2773), "Biosensors & Bioelectronics" (n=2684), and "Biomaterials" (n=2658) also garnered significant co-citation counts.

In terms of Total Link Strengths (TLSs), the list was notably similar to that of co-citations. "ACS Nano" led the field with a TLS of 465737, followed by "Advanced Materials" (n=392297), and "Angewandte Chemie-International Edition" (n=373159). The graphical representation in Figure S2D additionally revealed three distinct clusters, each colored differently to signify their thematic focus.

A scientometric analysis was executed using Citespace to examine the citation and co-citation patterns among references in the domain of nanozymes for cancer. The analysis yielded 151 references with more than 10 co-citations and 72 references with over 20 co-citations. These data were integrated into a co-citation network, visually represented in Fig. 3.

Inspection of Fig. 3 elucidates the temporal evolution of highly co-cited references spanning from 2009 to 2023, with a notable concentration in the years 2017–2021. Two distinct tables were generated to encapsulate this data. Table S8 outlines the top 10 most-cited references, whereas Table S9 elaborates on the top 10 most co-cited references within the field.

Remarkably, all listed references in Table S8 were original articles that contribute substantively to the field of nanozymes for cancer. The seminal work of MF Huo et al. [21] holds the highest citation count (n=895), succeeded by works from KL Fan et al. [6] (n=613), Y Zhang et al. [22] (n=547), W He et al. [23] (n=502), among others. Within this corpus, seven articles primarily focus on cancer treatment modalities and were published post 2017. Contrastingly, articles by W He et al. [23] and Y Tao et al. [24] pivot around in vitro cancer detection techniques and were disseminated earlier, in 2011 and 2013, respectively. Furthermore, the article by Y Hu et al. [25] offered an innovative approach to in vivo nanozyme-based cancer detection, published in 2017.

There were five review articles and five original articles in Table S9. The highly-cited reviews by JJX Wu et al. [26] and YY Huang et al. [27] were seminal works in the field. These reviews offer a comprehensive look at nanozymes, covering their classification, catalytic mechanisms, and applications in areas beyond cancer, such as biosensing and environmental protection. Their prominence in citation metrics underscores their importance in guiding research across various scientific domains. The most co-cited original article on nitrogen-doped carbon nanozyme for tumor catalytic therapy, belonged to KL Fan et al. [28] (n=183). Moreover, this article also gained the highest centrality (bc=0.12) among articles with more than 20 co-citations.

Utilizing CiteSpace, an extensive keyword assessment was executed, yielding a keyword set comprised of 670 terms interconnected through 3032 relationships, and a network density of 0.0135. Two metrics were employed for keyword evaluation: frequency and betweenness centrality. A compilation of these terms was tabulated in Table 1, featuring the 100 most recurrent keywords, and Table 2, which delineates the 38 keywords with a betweenness centrality exceeding 0.05. Notably, these tables reveal a prevalence of keywords pertinent to various forms of nanomaterials and nanostructures, including nanoparticles (n=522, bc=0.07), gold nanoparticles (n=150), metal organic frameworks (n=96, bc=0.05), graphene oxide (n=57), nanosheets (n=53), quantum dots (n=49), iron oxide nanoparticles (n=49, bc=0.1), nanocrystals (n=43, bc=0.08), graphene (n=40, bc=0.05), magnetic nanoparticles (n=30), nanocomposites (n=22), platinum nanoparticles (n=19, bc=0.09), graphene quantum dots (n=16), mesoporous silica nanoparticles (n=16, bc=0.09), carbon dots (n=12), carbon nanotubes(n=12), nanorods(n=10), gold nanoclusters (n=5, bc=0.07). Some keywords of common catalytic activities of nanozymes were also found, such as peroxidase-like activity (n=237, bc=0.1), hydrogen peroxide and H2O2 (n=157), peroxidase (n=47), oxidation (n=25), oxidase (n=13), superoxide dismutase (n=10). From the two primary domains of cancer research, we identified keywords directly related to cancer detection and treatment, respectively.

Cancer detection: colorimetric detection (n=109, bc=0.09), cancer cells (n=67, bc=0.05), signal amplification (n=34), assay (n=32), biosensor (n=30, bc=0.05), sensitive detection (n=27), electrochemical detection (n=18), ultrasensitive detection (n=18), immunoassay (n=18, bc=0.06), label free (n=16), immunosensor (n=15, bc=0.07), sensor (n=15), glucose detection (n=13), circulating tumor cells (n=11), carcinoembryonic antigen (n=10), colorimetric immunoassay (n=5, bc=0.07), biomarker (n=5, bc=0.05), tumor markers (n=4, bc=0.06).

Cancer treatment: photodynamic therapy (n=182, bc=0.08), photothermal therapy (n=125), drug delivery (n=85), cancer therapy (n=83), therapy (n=81), tumor microenvironment (n=75), chemodynamic therapy (n=69), sonodynamic therapy (n=42), resistance (n=27), synergistic therapy (n=20), tumor therapy (n=19), radiotherapy (n=17), chemotherapy (n=16).

Notably, the core keyword "nanozyme" (n=72) or "nanozymes" (n=79) were not the most frequently used. Only few detailed cancer types were found, namely breast cancer (n=22, bc=0.05), and prostate cancer (n=6, bc=0.06). Other vital high-frequency and high-centrality keywords included glucose (n=91), reactive oxygen species (n=85), oxidative stress (n=76, bc=0.07), hypoxia (n=73), oxygen (n=65, bc=0.07), DNA (n=41, bc=0.2), in vivo (n=37), apoptosis (n=33), tumor hypoxia (n=27), release (n=22), in vitro (n=18, bc=0.13), fenton reaction (n=11), cascade reaction (n=11).

Fig. 4A presents the clustering map generated after the application of latent semantic indexing and the assignment of indexing terms to clusters. This analysis serves as a foundational element for both the scientometric study at hand and subsequent timeline view analyses. There were 20 clusters in total, #0 reactive oxygen species, #1 nanoparticle, #2 colorimetric detection, #3 enzyme mimetics, #4 synergistic therapy, #5 cancer cells, #6 photodynamic therapy, #7 graphene, #8 mechanisms, #9 glucose, #10 magnetic nanoparticles, #11 fenton reaction, #12 iridium, #13 oxygen, #14 gold clusters, #15 single crystals, #16 gold-palladium hybrid nanostructures, #17 metal organic frameworks, #18 mass spectrometry, and #19 prostate cancer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Timeline, timezone, and landscape analyses of keywords
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequent to keyword clustering, timeline view analysis elucidated longevity and future potential of specific keyword clusters such as #0 reactive oxygen species, #4 synergistic therapy, #6 photodynamic therapy, #10 magnetic nanoparticles, and #11 Fenton reaction ( Fig. 5). Distinct from timeline view, timezone view-unconstrained by clustering-aligns nodes according to their initial appearance. Notably, sparse nodes appear post-2018 (Fig. 4B), with the majority concentrated between 2013 and 2016. This observation underscores the rapid advancements in nanozymes in cancer research during this timeframe, particularly in 2014. Complementary to this, the landscape analysis (Figure S3) summarizes key developments within each keyword cluster. Collectively, the prime evolutionary stages across clusters were primarily located in the years ranging from 2012 to 2017, corroborating the findings from the timezone view.

The utility of burst analysis, facilitated by Citespace, offers an opportunity to identify rapidly evolving keywords within a defined temporal window, providing a snapshot of emerging trends in the research landscape. A total of 53 burst keywords were identified for the period extending from 2011 to 2023. We opted to highlight 35 of these, distinguished by their exceptional citation burst strength ( Fig. 6).

Fig. 6 showed that the time span of each keyword's burst activity was defined by its initial and terminal years, and was accentuated by a prominent red liner segment [29]. Keywords such as "Signal amplification" and "tumor microenvironment" (TME) demonstrated the highest citation bursts with strengths of 12.39 (2016–2019) and 11.03 (2021–2023), respectively. Other notable keywords showcasing substantial citation bursts include "Graphene oxide" (s=8.45), "Gold nanoparticles" (s=7.91), and "Artificial enzymes" (s=7.85).

In terms of burst duration, "Immunoassay" sustained its burst status for an impressive nine years, spanning 2011–2019. Similar longevity was observed for keywords such as "Glucose detection", "Electrochemical detection" and "Immunosensor".

Notably, the current evolving trends in the field were exemplified by recently bursting keywords, encompassing terms like "Resistance", "Tumor hypoxia", "Nanozyme", "Synergistic therapy" and "Chemodynamic therapy". Furthermore, the burst analysis offers temporal insights into the research focus on cancer detection and treatment. Prior to 2017, the bursting keywords predominantly pertained to in vitro cancer detection. However, after 2018, a shift was evident towards keywords related to in vivo cancer treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overall scientometric analysis of nanozymes in the field of cancer/tumor
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the research progress and emerging trends of nanozymes in cancer/tumor research, we conducted this scientometric analysis of related publications from WoSCC, timeline of research development, list of influential researchers, research institutes and influential journals were obtained. First, the annual distribution of 1496 papers showed that research on nanozymes in the field of cancer has obtained a lot of attention, especially after 2017. Studies on nanozymes for cancer detection was conducted earlier than those for cancer treatment. Theranostic nanozymes integrating cancer treatment and diagnosis have also obtained a lot of attention in the last five years. Among 59 countries participating in this field, China had the most publications. Consistent with this, the top 20 institutions with the most publications were all Chinese institutions, indicating the dominant role of Chinese researchers in this field. The Chinese Academy of Sciences was the top institution with the most publications and highest centrality. For subject categories of this field, "Nanoscience & Nanotechnology", "Materials Science, Multidisciplinary", and "Chemistry, Multidisciplinary" were the top ones with the highest frequencies. Then the authors with high publication numbers, high citations and co-citations were identified, respectively. From 2009–2023, PP Yang had the most relevant publications, followed by XG Qu, Y Chen, J Lin, JS Ren, KL Fan, and Y Zhang. XG Qu and JS Ren emerged as the foremost authors in terms of citation counts, succeeded by Y Chen, JL Shi, J Lin, KL Fan, XY Yan, LZ Gao, and others. In the realm of co-citations, LZ Gao led the field, followed by Y Zhang, Y Liu, and JJX Wu, and others. For authors and references, highly cited or co-cited authors typically have one or several highly cited or co-cited articles. They might be pioneers in a particular field, such as Fan et al. with his work on nanozyme catalytic therapy [6], or they could be consistently contributing to the field of nanozymes. The consistent production of high-quality articles usually indicates the strong capabilities of the research group. Additionally, continual innovation and exploration of unknown territories in nanozymes are also crucial factors, as seen in Fan group’s work on natural biogenic nanozyme in 2024 [30]. An examination was also conducted on journal publications, citations, and co-citations. The journal "ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces" featured the most articles in this field from 2009 to 2023, followed by "Biosensors & Bioelectronics", "Journal of Materials Chemistry B" and "Chemical Engineering Journal" Moreover, "ACS Nano", "Advanced Materials" and "Angewandte Chemie-International Edition" were the top three journals with both the highest citations and highest co-citations. For fields and journals, the nanozyme field is an interdisciplinary one, primarily involving chemistry and materials science (nanomaterials). This defines the research domain. Regarding citations and co-citations of journals, they depend partly on the journals' publication volume, quality, and relevance to the field, and partly on the significance of the published research. For example, "ACS Nano", "Advanced Materials" and "Angewandte Chemie-International Edition" are high-quality journals in the materials and chemistry fields, and important research is often published in these journals. We also conducted a reference analysis, which specified the key documents that have greatly promoted the development of this field. Especially for nanozymes used for cancer treatment, these pioneering original experiments laid firm foundations for later studies. Furthermore, the examination of keywords within this domain constitutes the central focus of this manuscript. As such, pertinent discussions will be incorporated into a detailed and exhaustive discourse on the field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Classifications of nanozymes in cancer diagnosis and treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes are defined as nanomaterials with enzyme-like characteristics [1,2]. Based on the definition, there are two main strategies for classifications of nanozymes [1,2,10]. One is based on nanomaterials constituting nanozymes. And the other is based on catalytic activities of nanozymes.
According to the different chemical types of nanomaterials, this review categorizes them into four types: metal-based, carbon-based, organic nanozymes and composite nanozymes.

Up to now, metal-based nanozymes are the most common candidates of nanozymes in the field of cancer detection and treatment [31–36], which can also be indicated from our scientometric analysis. Gold nanoparticles and metal organic frameworks (MOFs) were the top two keywords among all nano-related high-frequency keywords. Iron oxide nanoparticles, magnetic nanoparticles, platinum nanoparticles, and gold nanoclusters were also among the high-frequency keywords, signifying their relevance and utility in this domain. In addition, mesoporous silica nanoparticles emerged not only as a high-frequency keyword but also as a term of high centrality and a burst keyword. Mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) are valued in the realm of drug delivery due to their expansive surface areas, customizable geometries, and dimensions, along with their substantial loading capacities [37,38]. Moreover, MSNs are also useful nanocarriers to load specific nanozymes with smaller sizes [39–43]. Metal-based nanozymes can be further classified into monometal, metal alloy, metal oxides, metal chalcogenides, metal hydroxides, metal ions, metal hybrid, MOFs, or complexes (such as Prussian blue, heme) [32,44,45]. In 2007, Yan Group discovered the peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic NPs (Fe3O4) for the first time, which set a precedent for nanozymes. Among the ten most-cited references within this field, nine of them were related to metal-based nanozymes [5]. Until now, hundreds of metal-based nanozymes have been discovered and used in cancer diagnosis and treatment, such as iron and iron oxides [46–50], cobalt oxides [51,52], copper and copper oxide [53–56], cerium oxides [34,57], manganese oxides [58,59], vanadium oxides [60,61], nickel [62], and noble metals including gold (Au) [63,64], palladium (Pd) [65,66], platinum [67,68], and silver [69,70]. These metal-based nanozymes are found to demonstrate various kinds of catalytic activities, including oxidoreductases (for example, peroxidase, oxidase, catalase, and superoxide dismutase) [71,72], hydrolases (for example, nuclease [73]) and lyases (for example, photolyases [74]). Moreover, many metal-based nanozymes such as Pd, Au, CeO2, Fe3O4, Mn2O3, Prussian blue nanozyme can reveal multienzyme-like activities, indicating their potential wide application in cancer detection and treatment [32]. Based on the extensive research on metal-based nanozymes, several comprehensive reviews focusing on metal-based nanozymes were made recently [32,44,75].

Carbon-based nanozymes are also commonly used in cancer detection and cancer treatment. In 2018, Fan et al. designed a nitrogen-doped porous carbon nanospheres with four enzyme-like activities (oxidase, peroxidase, catalase, and superoxide dismutase) for tumor catalytic therapy [6]. This study emerged as the sole work focusing on carbon-based nanozymes among the top ten most-cited references in this field. Our scientometric analysis also identified a series of high-frequency keywords, including graphene oxide, quantum dots, graphene, graphene quantum dots, carbon dots, and carbon nanotubes. Carbon-based nanozymes can be classified into fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon dots (including graphene quantum dots, carbon quantum dots, and carbon nanodots) [45,76]. They can mimic catalytic activities of peroxidases and superoxide dismutases, as well as oxidases (laccase) and catalases [45,76]. Besides, there are several strategies to enhance catalytic activities of nanozymes, such as heteroatomic doped nanozymes and carbon-based hybrid nanozymes. Heteroatomic doping can enhance the intrinsic catalytic activities of nanozymes [77–80], and the carbon-based nanomaterials can provide a large specific surface area and excellent physical and chemical properties to disperse and stabilize other nanozymes, modulate substrate adsorption, and enhance the synergetic effects [45,81,82]. There are several specific reviews on carbon-based nanozymes in recent years [45,76,83].

When referring to organic nanozymes, MOFs are the first to be considered because of their wide application in this field [84–88]. Single organic nanomaterials acting as nanozymes are much fewer than the former metal-based and carbon-based nanozymes or MOFs. And to our knowledge, only several types of organic polymers have been used as nanozymes for cancer detection or treatment, such as poly (pyrrole) [89–92], poly (N-phenylglycine) [93,94], and polythiophene [95,96]. Besides, considering the current research status of organic nanozymes, a comprehensive review containing mechanisms, classification, applications is probably needed.

Of late, single-atom nanozymes (SAzymes), featuring isolated metal atoms stabilized on nitrogen-doped carbon substrates, have garnered considerable interest in the arena of oncological diagnostics and therapeutics [97–99]. SAzymes, endowed with uniformly distributed active sites, boast nearly complete atom utilization efficiency along with peak specificity, thereby underscoring their advantageous catalytic performance and selectivity [100].

In practical applications, nanozymes are often surface-modified or cross-linked with natural enzymes, drugs, or key regulatory factors to enhance their catalytic activities, achieve targeted therapy, or enable multi-pathway cascade treatments. These nano-based catalytic materials with composite structures or functions are referred to as composite nanozymes. For example, Gui et al. designed a closed cascade catalytic system called MSN@Cu-CeO2@GOx, composed of a Cu-CeO2 nanozyme with catalase-like (CAT) activity and a natural enzyme (GOx). The catalytic product of natural GOx, H2O2, serves as the substrate for the Cu-CeO2 nanozyme, enabling continuous glucose consumption and gradual deoxygenation in the tumor for starvation therapy [101]. Li et al. created a nanozyme complex (DOX@CuMn-DAzymes) loaded with the chemotherapeutic drug doxorubicin. This complex enhances intracellular singlet oxygen (1O2) levels while silencing P-glycoprotein and heat shock proteins, effectively overcoming therapeutic resistance through synergistic treatment [102]. For surface modifications, Zhao et al. utilized platinum nanozyme micelle cascade enzyme activity to modify homologous tumor cell membranes (DC@Pt), generating a redox reaction biomimetic nanoplatform (DPC@Pt@M) for targeted colon cancer therapy [103]. In another study, Chen et al. developed a cascade nanozyme (CuPy-Au@EM) that mimics tumor cell exosomes for targeted breast cancer radiotherapy. The exosomal membrane proteins on the surface allow precise localization of the multifunctional nanozyme at the tumor site [104]. Due to the unique multifunctionality of composite nanozymes, this field has seen rapid development in recent years.

This classification seems more popular to use in the field of cancer/tumor, probably because catalytic activities of nanozymes are more associated with the practical applications [10,105]. Up to now, nanozymes have been found to be able to mimic over 30 types of catalytic activities, mainly including oxidoreductases, hydrolases, lyases [2,106]. Among all kinds of catalytic activities, oxidoreductase family is most studied, which constitutes the majority of nanozyme-related research. Oxidoreductase family can be further classified into peroxidase (POD)-like activity, glutathione (GSH) peroxidase (GPx)-like activity, catalase (CAT)-like activity, oxidase (OXD)-like activity, superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity, etc [107].

POD-like activity holds the distinction of being both the inaugural and the most extensively researched catalytic function among nanozymes in the oxidoreductase family [5,106]. The scientometric analysis conducted in our study also revealed that POD-like activity served as a keyword of both high frequency and high centrality. POD-like nanozymes can catalyze the oxidation of substrate in the presence of peroxide [108]. The combination of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is commonly employed to assess both the presence and intensity of this catalytic reaction [109]. This combination is widely used in POD-like nanozymes-mediated colorimetric analysis. Moreover, the intermediate •OH (hydroxyl radicals, a type of reactive oxygen species (ROS) with strong cytotoxicity) of POD-like nanozymes-mediated catalytic reaction have also been widely used in ROS increase-mediated cancer treatment [109].

GPx serves as a pivotal component within the cellular antioxidant system, since it can catalyze the transformation of H2O2/organic hydroperoxides to H2O/alcohols, with GSH transferring to glutathione disulfide [110]. Moreover, GPx can also cause GSH depletion to increase ROS level, which is commonly used in nanozymes for cancer treatment [111–115]. In this situation, GPx is usually combined with other catalytic activities in the nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms, playing an assistant role [111–115]. Besides, Lai et al. recently reviewed the mechanism, classification, and bioapplication of GPx-like nanozymes, which classified GPx-like nanozymes into five types, including selenium (Ce)-based, vanadium (V)-based, manganese (Mn)-based, copper (Cu)-based, and others (such as Au-based, Fe-based, or multiple active centers-based) based on the active center elements of nanozymes [116].

CAT-like nanozymes can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into molecular water (H2O) and oxygen (O2). Therefore, the two main strategies of CAT-like nanozymes include acting as antioxidant to decrease ROS level, and acting as O2 generator to increase O2. While the former role is beneficial in the field of anti-inflammatory therapy and cytoprotection, the latter role as O2 generator of CAT-like nanozymes is greatly useful for cancer treatment [117]. It is widely acknowledged that hypoxia in TME is a substantial obstacle for various cancer treatment approaches, resulting in drug resistance, progression, and recurrence [118–120]. Therefore, targeting hypoxia and increasing oxygen supply in TME are a useful strategy to enhance the efficacy of cancer therapies such as chemotherapy, radiotherapy, immunotherapy, photodynamic therapy, and sonodynamic therapy [121–124]. Xu et al. published a review on CAT-like nanozymes in 2022, which summarized their classification, catalytic mechanisms, and applications [117].

OXD-like nanozymes usually catalyze oxidation-reduction reactions at the expense of O2, which is transferred to H2O2, H2O, or O2 •- [10,125]. Therefore, OXD-like nanozymes are often used to increase ROS level to enhance antitumor effects in cancer treatment. In addition, nanozymes with OXD-like activity are also applicable for the identification of cancer cells or biomarkers [58, 126–129]. Glucose oxidase (Gox) is an example of oxidase, which can catalyze the transformation of glucose to gluconic acid, with the result of glucose consumption and tumor inhibition [130]. This GOx-based treatment strategy is one of the mostly used strategies of starvation therapies. Besides the intrinsic GOx-mimicking activity of nanozymes, GOx can also be conjugated onto or loaded into nanozymes to form nanozyme-based nanoplatforms [131–134]. GSH oxidase (GSHOx)-like nanozyme is another commonly studied OXD-like nanozymes, which can play the role of GSH depletion [135–137]. Other oxidases used in nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms include lactate oxidase [138], NADH oxidase [139], cholesterol oxidase [140], and cytochrome c oxidase [141]. Moreover, these nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms with OXD-like or OXD activities are often used to produce H2O2 in situ in the TME, providing ingredients for further performance of POD- or CAT-like activities of nanozymes. Thus, OXD-like activity is vital in the cascade reactions systems of nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms.

Nanozymes with SOD-like capabilities facilitate the dismutation of the superoxide radical (O2 •-) into molecular oxygen (O2) and H2O2. This functionality enables them to serve as antioxidants, mitigating oxidative stress and thereby safeguarding cellular integrity. Similar to the role of CAT, producing O2 and improving hypoxic TME are also one of the most important roles of SOD-like nanozymes. In 2021, Zhao et al. reviewed the recent advances of SOD-like nanozymes, with the summary of the structure, regulation, measurement and application of SOD-like nanozymes [142].

Bioorthogonal chemistry was firstly proposed by Bertozzi Group in 2003, and it has experienced enormous development in the last two decades [143]. Bioorthogonal reactions are special reactions that neither occur in biology nor interfere with naturally occurring processes, ensuring the safety and feasibility of in vivo antitumor therapies [144–146]. Generally, bio-orthogonal reactions can be classified into metal-catalyzed reactions and metal-free reactions. They mainly involve the following reactions: Staudinger ligation, copper catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition, strain promoted azide-alkyne cycloaddition, photo-/thermo-induced thiol-ene coupling reaction, inverse electron demand Diels-Alder reaction, hydrazino-Pictet-Spengler ligation, Michael additions, etc. [144,145]. In 2021 Fedeli et al. proposed a more intuitive classification of bio-orthogonal transforming reactions [146], including uncaging reactions, coupling reactions, and intramolecular reactions. Inspired by other catalytic activities of nanozymes and the development of nanotechnology, nanomaterials-based bio-orthogonal catalytic activity has been well studied in recent years. In particular, transition metal catalysts (TMCs) encapsulated in nanomaterial scaffolds have been widely studied in biological applications including cancer detection and treatment [144,146]. TMCs mainly include Ru, Pd, Ir, Pt, Cu, Fe, Au, etc., whereas common nanomaterial scaffolds include MOFs, Au NPs, silica NPs, resin microspheres, ferritin nanocages, etc. [144,146,147].

Recently, novel nanozymes with multi-enzyme-mimicking functions have been explored well in cancer diagnosis and treatment [148]. This research trend is in accord with clinical practice of cancer management, namely adopting combined strategies. Moreover, explorations of nanozymes with multi-functions also promote the development of nanozyme-based theranostic nanoplatforms, which are probably a trend of this field.

With the discovery of more and more nanozymes, it is necessary to stress the importance of calculating and comparing their catalytic activity and kinetics. It is widely acknowledged that nanozymes rely on the intrinsic surface of NPs for catalysis [5,149]. Moreover, the kinetics of nanozyme reactions are generally fitted with the Michaelis-Menten kinetic model. And the several common comparison methods include Michaelis constant (K M), maximum velocity (V max), and catalytic turnover rate (k cat). K M and V max of nanozymes can be obtained through plotting the reaction rate versus substrate concentration. K M of a certain nanozyme (under same pH, temperature, and ionic strength) is a constant, which can measure the affinity between a nanozyme and its substrate. In addition, V max is reached when the active sites are saturated with substrate. k cat is also a key constant, which is obtained after V max is divided by the concentration of nanozymes [149]. Moreover, k cat reveals the rate at which nanozymes catalyze substrate formation under optimal conditions. Besides, the standardized assays of catalytic activity and kinetics of nanozymes are also important for basic and application research including cancer research, as well as industrialization [108].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of nanozymes in cancer detection
ce:section.ce:para: In terms of cancer detection, it can be classified into three stages, 1) cancer screening among individuals at high risk for cancer, 2) cancer diagnosis, and 3) cancer surveillance for patients already diagnosed with cancer, though cancer diagnosis is a more commonly used keyword to describe cancer detection for nanozymes [150,151]. Tumor biopsy is the gold standard for the diagnosis of most cancers [152]. But it is very complex and inconvenient, unsuitable for early screening, and inevitably bringing patients a lot of pain. Therefore, more convenient detection technologies are being developed. Under this circumstance, nanozymes with high sensitivity and specificity are regarded as suitable candidates for cancer detection [153]. Of note, with the popular concept of early cancer detection, diagnostic nanozymes have become a significant area of interest, which can be revealed by our scientometric examination. Both "colorimetric detection" and "cancer cells" emerged not merely as keywords with high frequencies and betweenness centralities, but also as clustering keywords, thereby underscoring their significance in this domain. Other high frequency keywords related to cancer detection included "signal amplification", "biosensor", "sensitive detection", "electrochemical detection", "ultrasensitive detection", "immunoassay", "immunosensor", "sensor", "glucose detection", "circulating tumor cells", "carcinoembryonic antigen", "colorimetric immunoassay", "biomarker" and "tumor markers". Besides, the burst analysis of keywords indicated that nanozymes used for cancer detection, especially in vitro cancer detection, have been explored and studied in an early time.

Owing to the low cost, high stability, high catalytic efficiency, and ease of modification, nanozymes have been widely used in the field of in vitro cancer detections [164]. Table 3 listed the recent applications of nanozymes for cellular and molecular biomarker detections in the last four years.

Based on detection principles and methods, in vitro detections can be generally classified into four types: biochemical detection, immunoassay, molecular diagnostics, and microbial detection. For nanozymes used for in vitro cancer detection, biochemical detection and immunoassay are the main approaches [165,166]. Biochemical detection is a wide concept in the medical science, and in this section nanozyme-based colorimetric analysis without using immunoassay or aptasensor is regarded as the main biochemical detection approach. Immunoassay constitutes a highly discriminating method of detection, predicated on the immunological interaction between specific antigens and their corresponding antibodies. Up to now, approximately 50 % of nanozyme-based in vitro cancer diagnostics were conducted utilizing immunoassay techniques ( Table 3 ). Hence, immunoassays and non-immunoassays were used as the first criteria for classification of in vitro cancer detections ( Fig. 7A). There are different schemes of immunoassays, among which sandwich and competitive binding methods are most popular [164]. Based on labeling objects on the antigens or antibodies, immunoassays were classified into radiolabeled immunoassay (RIA), enzyme immunoassay (EIA), and enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). Then more signal generators were invented based on EIA/ELISA, and immunoassays have further expanded from colorimetric immunoassay to different forms with various detection signals, such as fluorescence immunoassay (FIA), chemiluminescent immunoassay (CLIA), electrochemical immunoassay (ECIA), electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (ECLIA), photoelectrochemical immunoassay (PECIA), etc. [107,167]. Besides, these signal generating approaches can also be used in non-immunoassay-based cancer detections. Nevertheless, EIA/ELISA continue to remain prominent in the field of immunoassays [164].

However, biological enzymes labeled on antigens or antibodies are easily inactivated under non-physiological conditions such as inappropriate pH and temperature. The extraction and purification processes of biological enzymes are expensive with strict technical requirements. Hence, nanozymes, distinguished by their elevated stability, efficiency, and cost-effectiveness, are considered promising technologies to ameliorate the inherent limitations of biological enzymes. These attributes hold the potential to enhance the sensitivity and accuracy of EIA/ELISA. For example, horseradish peroxidase (HRP) which is widely used in ELISA can be replaced by POD-like nanozymes, and the fabricated nanozyme-linked immunosorbent assay (NLISA) enables the sensitive detection of cancer cells and biomarkers. Besides nanozyme-based immunoassays and immunosensors, nanozyme-based aptasensors have also been well studied [154]. An aptamer, comprising a truncated oligonucleotide sequence (either DNA or RNA), presents a viable alternative to antibodies in the context of molecular recognition. Aptamers are advantageous due to their superior stability and cost-efficacy. Remarkably, these synthetic oligonucleotide ligands often match or even surpass the sensitivity of their protein-based counterparts, thereby broadening the scope of their applicability in diagnostic and therapeutic modalities. Therefore, nanozyme-based aptasensors are useful and powerful tools for in vitro cancer detections.

In addition, interactions between nanozymes and DNA or specific analytes can also influence the detection process. Appropriate use of these interactions may help develop novel detection approaches or improve the current detection approaches [164]. Molecular diagnostics involves detections or recognitions of DNA or RNA, and its traditional approaches include nucleic acid amplification techniques (e.g., polymerase chain reaction, nucleic acid hybridization techniques (e.g., fluorescence in situ hybridization, DNA microarrays (gene chips), etc. Besides, CRISPR/CAS system-mediated molecular diagnostics is also becoming a novel detection tool. Applications of nanozymes alone in molecular diagnostics are relatively less, compared with biochemical detection and immunoassay. Nevertheless, detection approaches of different classifications can have a cross, connection, or combination, which can promote the expansion of detection objects and increase the sensitivity and specificity [168–170]. Recently, there were several studies based on CRISPR/CAS system-mediated molecular diagnostics, which combined with other detection approaches to achieve the detection of some noncoding RNAs [168,171].

As the scientometric analysis and Table 3 represented, colorimetric detection is the most common strategy for in vitro cancer detections. About a half of related studies in Table 3 were associated with colorimetric detection. Most of these studies were based on POD-like nanozymes, with a couple of OXD or CAT-like nanozymes. As an illustrative example, POD-like nanozymes function through a two-step catalytic process. Initially, these nanozymes cleave the peroxide bond (O-O) of H2O2, yielding •OH that possess substantial oxidative capacity. Subsequently, the generated •OH radicals proceed to oxidize chromogenic substrates, thereby producing chromogenic products. Consequently, colorimetric detection hinges on the chromatic transition of specific chromogenic substrates. This methodology can achieve both qualitative and quantitative assessment of the target analyte, either directly or through the medium of signal amplification, thereby inducing a noticeable alteration in color. TMB is the most used chromogenic substrates for colorimetric detection. Other possible substrates include 2,2′-azino-bis-3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid, o-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride, di-azo-aminobenzene. Table 3 also shows the several commonly detected objects including cancer cells especially CTCs, tumor cell-derived exosomes, and specific tumor antigens and cancer-related nucleic acid (DNA/RNA). Moreover, since detecting a series of biomarkers of a certain cancer increases the accuracy and sensitivity, the multi-detection approach such as multi-analyte immunoassays and immunosensing arrays are becoming an interesting and promising field [70,164].

In recent years, there were many studies using CRISPR/Cas systems to detect DNA/RNA [168, 190–194], and several of them combined with nanozyme-based catalytic activities [168,171]. In a seminal study published in 2022, Broto et al. delineated a pioneering methodology leveraging nanozyme-facilitated CRISPR/Cas13 for the development of a colorimetric immunoassay, referred to as "CrisprZyme" [168]. This innovative approach enables the detection of non-coding RNAs in various pathological conditions, including neoplastic diseases, without the prerequisite for preamplification ( Fig. 8A). In this CRISPR/Cas13-based NLISA, the bimetallic Pt@Au was chosen as POD-like nanozyme [195]. It was empirically determined that the assay exhibited a limit of detection (LOD) for RNA concentration significantly surpassing the sensitivity of previously documented Cas13 assays - manifested by a LOD as low as 4.72 pM - when quantified through colorimetric change using a plate reader at an absorbance of 450 nm (A450). The dynamic range for the limit of LOD in the CrisprZyme assay was determined to be 7.8±3.21 pM, a performance metric that stands in comparative alignment with other amplification-free methodologies reliant on electrochemical readouts for detection. Besides, different Cas enzymes including LwaCas13a and LbuCas13a can be combined with CrisprZyme, and LbuCas13a-based CrisprZyme increased the LOD to 264.2±0.16 fM. Utilizing an integrated approach comprising reverse transcription recombinase polymerase amplification (RT-RPA), T7-based RNA transcription, Cas13 detection, and Nanoluciferase-based immunoassay (NLISA), the sensitivity of the CrisprZyme assay was markedly elevated, achieving a LOD of 8.30±0.97 fM. Notably, the assay was employed to quantify the expression of circular AURKA RNA (circ-AURKA) in two pathologically distinct variants of prostate cancer: neuroendocrine prostate carcinoma (NEPC) and prostate adenocarcinoma (ACA). Circ-AURKA has been identified as a biomarker specific to NEPC, showing negligible expression levels in ACA. Consequently, a specialized qualitative assay was developed for circ-AURKA in NEPC and ACA samples, leveraging RT-RPA and T7-based transcription for preamplification, followed by detection using the CrisprZyme-based immunoassay (Fig. 8B). Finally, circ-AURKA was detected positive in nine of ten NEPC biopsies, and one of ten ACA biopsies (Fig. 8C), supporting CrisprZyme to serve as a noncoding RNA detector as well as provide evidence for cancer classification.

Recently, Yang et al. [154] introduced a photothermal cytosensor designed for the detection of breast cancer circulating tumor cells (BC-CTCs). This innovative system involved the utilization of aptamer-functionalized Fe3O4@polydopamine (PDA) nanoparticles for the specific recognition of BC-CTCs. To enhance signal amplification, Ti3C2@Au@Pt nanozymes were incorporated into the sensor ( Fig. 9A). The choice of MCF-7 cells was made to model BC-CTCs, as these cells exhibit elevated levels of certain molecules, such as MUC1, vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF165), and epithelial cell-adhesion molecule (EpCAM). This study had four types of DNA sequences (P1, P2, P3, P4). P1, P2 and P3 DNA sequences contained MUC1, VEGF165, and EpCAM aptamers, respectively. P4 DNA sequence played the role of inducing the strand displacement reaction of P1. P1 was modified on the surface of Fe3O4@PDA NPs (Fe3O4@PDA-P1) to achieve effective capture of BC-CTCs. After magnetic separation, the Fe3O4@PDA-P1-conjugated BC-CTCs were separated from samples and then conjugated with Ti3C2@Au@Pt-P2 and Ti3C2@Au@Pt-P3. In this chemical pathway, upon the introduction of P4, a strand displacement reaction ensues, resulting in the liberation of Fe3O4@PDA-P1 from the entrapped BC-CTCs. The incorporation of magnetic separation techniques and the subsequent introduction of TMB serve to further refine the experimental parameters. Then the Ti3C2@Au@Pt-P2, P3-conjugated BC-CTCs can oxidize TMB to produce oxTMB, which enhances the photothermal effect [196]. Owing to the photothermal capabilities of the Ti3C2@Au@Pt nanozyme and the amplification of the photothermal effect mediated by TMB oxidation, the temperature of the solution increased upon exposure to near-infrared (NIR) light. Hence, BC-CTCs could be detected based on the temperature change. The feasibility, sensitivity and specificity of this photothermal cytosensor were tested. The temperature changes of MCF-7 cells-absent, Ti3C2@Au@Pt-P2, Ti3C2@Au@Pt-P3, and Ti3C2@Au@Pt-P1, P2 groups were found at 3.6℃, 15.5℃, 14.3℃, and 22.1℃, respectively (Fig. 9B). Additionally, the photothermal cytosensor exhibited noticeable color changes, discernible only in the infrared thermal images (Fig. 9C), indicating its feasibility for BC-CTCs detection. The relationship between temperature change (ΔT) and the logarithmic concentration of MCF-7 cells was examined, revealing a robust linear correlation in the concentration range of 50–1.0×105 cells mL−1. Simultaneously, a gradual rise in the intensity values of thermal images was noted, correlating with increasing concentrations of MCF-7 cells (Fig. 9D). Furthermore, the photothermal cytosensor exhibited robust selectivity for the detection of MCF-7 cells, likely attributed to the utilization of three distinct types of aptamers.

Overall, Yang et al. proposed a feasible strategy to achieve cancer cell detection with good sensitivity and selectivity. Employing photothermal analysis into the field of in vitro cancer detection can avoid and improve the disadvantages of current detection approaches (such as immunoassay). The detection objects can probably be expanded to other cancer cells or specific types of cells after simply changing suitable aptamers. However, the LOD and the liner range of this detection approach could not catch up with some other methods, which indicated the necessity of improving the photothermal and catalysis performances of nanozymes as well as the separation ability with high accuracy and efficiency of magnetic materials. Moreover, combining this photothermal analysis-based detection approach with other useful detection approaches, such as immunoassay, may also be a promising way to enhance the sensitivity and selectivity.

In vivo cancer detection mainly refers to in vivo cancer imaging, with the employment of various imaging techniques such as fluorescence imaging (FLI) (e.g., near infrared FLI (NIRFLI)), ultrasound imaging (USI), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), photoacoustic imaging (PAI), photothermal imaging (PTI), computed tomography (CT), bioluminescence imaging (BL), chemiluminescence (CL), etc. (Fig. 7B). And nanozymes used for in vivo cancer detections can play the role by either probes or contrast agents. The quality and performance of in vivo cancer imaging mainly depend on the physicochemical property and catalytic activity of nanozymes [10,197]. Compared with in vitro cancer detections, in vivo cancer detections face much more challenges before success. The intricate TME, which was a keyword with both high frequency and high betweenness centrality, plays a great role in this process. The TME mainly consists of cancer cells with high heterogeneity and other different types of cells such as immune cells, matrix cells, and a lot of changing physiological conditions and chemical factors [198,199]. Therefore, the designation of nanozymes for in vivo cancer imaging is more complex, with higher requirements of good biocompatibility, stability, excellent catalytic activity, and targeting ability. Moreover, most recent studies focus on the construction of nanozyme-based theranostic platform with both cancer imaging and cancer therapy (imaging-guided cancer therapy). In 2023, Dong et al. [197] reviewed the recent applications of nanozymes in imaging-monitored cancer therapy. In this review, four types of imaging methods including fluorescent probes, chemiluminescent probes, MRI contrast agents, and photoacoustic imaging probes and their representative studies were well discussed. However, it would be better if more imaging methods are included and a detailed summary or list of recent related studies should be made. Therefore, this review plans to provide a summary of nanozyme-based in vivo cancer detection containing both imaging-guided cancer therapy (theranostic nanozymes) and single cancer imaging.

Table 4 summarized studies on nanozymes used for in vivo cancer detections in recent four years. Around half of the incorporated studies adopted a single-mode approach. And the remaining half were adopting multi-modes approaches including dual-modes, triple-modes, and four-modes. Among various detection approaches, MRI, FLI, and PAI are most frequently used in this field. Different from in vitro cancer detections, catalysis activities of nanozymes used for in vivo cancer detections were richer. There were more applications of nanozymes with multi-enzyme-mimicking functions. This may be associated with the complex TME under in vivo detections. Moreover, most nanozymes used for in vivo cancer detections were also used for cancer therapies, acting as theranostic nanoplatforms. Nevertheless, an ideal nanozyme-based theranostic nanoplatform is still on the way.
