Overall, an ideal nanozyme-based theranostic platform, which is characterized by biocompatibility, targeting ability, responsiveness, controlled activation, antitumor effects, and real-time imaging, is required in the future.

In 2021, Teng et al. designed an acidic TME-responsive nanozyme platform (MSPN) with applications of real-time NIRFLI and PTI for predicting therapeutic effects [203]. NIRFLI showcases remarkable sensitivity and swift response, whereas PTI excels in providing exceptional imaging depth and spatial resolution in vivo [203,209,210]. Moreover, ratiometric method was used to improve the accuracy, sensitivity, and reliability of the dual-model imaging [211]. The MSPN nanoplatform combines mixed-valent manganese oxide (MnOx) and a semiconducting polymer (PFODBT), connected via an amide bond to an oxidase-responsive molecule (ORM) ( Fig. 10). When ORM undergoes degradation by MnOx-generated MnIII (depending on the OXD-like activity) under acidic conditions, the NIRFLI and PAI imaging signals at specific wave lengths and their ratiometric signals will be changed. In our investigation, the MSPN displayed a reduction in emission at 825 nm and an elevation at 695 nm for NIRFAI. Concurrently, the internal reference signal of PFODBT remained nearly constant at 680 nm, while the responsive signal from ORM decreased at 780 nm. Therefore, the ratiometric NIRFLI and PAI signal outputs of MSPN were finally recognized as FL695/FL825 and PA680/PA780, respectively. Based on the detection of FL695/FL825 and PA680/PA780, MSPN can achieve the real-time monitoring of its own OXD-like activity and cancer catalytic treatment (Fig. 10Bi). Both in vitro and in vivo studies verified the feasibility and reliability of MSPN for real-time monitoring of OXD-like activity (Fig. 10Bii-Bx). Further, the in vivo study of MSPN for cancer therapy and its monitoring was conducted (Fig. 10 Ci-Cvii). Mice bearing 4T1 breast cancer xenografts were intravenously injected (i.v.) with MSPN or SPN (PFODBT-ORM). The FLI intensity ratios (FL695/FL825) and PAI intensity ratios (PA680/PA780) of tumor areas in the MSPN group showed a significant increase over time, compared to those in the SPN group (Fig. 10 Cii-Cv). Among the four groups of mice treated with PBS (i.t.), SPN (i.t.), MSPN (i.t.), and MSPN (i.v.), a notable inhibition of tumor growth was observed in the MSPN and MSPN groups (Fig. 10 Cvi), which was consistent with OXD activity detections under NIRFLI and PAI (Fig. 10 Civ, Cv). In addition, hematoxylin-eosin staining of tumor slices also verified that there were significantly more necrotic areas in MSPN and MSPN groups. Overall, this study provided a novel nanozyme-based nanoplatform with dual NIRFLI and PTI imaging for monitoring the catalytic activity and catalytic activity-mediated treatment. Nevertheless, inclusion of orthotopic transplantation tumor experiments in this study may be more reliable to mimic the actual TME of breast cancer in vivo and verify the dual model monitoring process. Targeting modifications of nanozyme-based nanoplatform may further enhance the imaging performance, as well as reduce the dosage of the agents. Besides, the therapeutic effect of this nanoplatform was mainly from nanozymes-mediated ROS increase. Therefore, the therapeutic effect may be enhanced if combined with other feasible therapies, especially NIR light-related therapies. More importantly, a further consideration of whether the in vivo combined therapies can still be efficiently monitored by this nanoplatform is necessary.

In 2022, Chen et al. proposed a H2O2, acidity-dual responsive theranostics nanoplatform (Fe3O4/TMB-PEG-RGD, FTRNPs) with the ability of three-model imaging (FLI, PAI, PTI) and combined photothermal therapy (PTT)/ chemodynamic therapy (CDT) for cancers [162]. FTRNPs were characterized by dual-targeting ability, based on the modification of RGD peptides and physically magnetic targeting of Fe3O4 NPs ( Fig. 11A). RGD peptides exhibit specific binding affinity towards the overexpressed αvβ3 integrin found on the membranes of tumor cells. Upon infiltration into tumor sites, facilitated by their dual targeting capabilities, FTRNPs were subsequently activated to enhance the precision of PTT. This activation was contingent on the heightened levels of H2O2 and acidity in the TME. Besides, POD-like nanozyme-mediated CDT also enhanced the antitumor effects of FTRNPs. In this study, three imaging approaches including FLI, PAI, PTI were employed. The initial focus of our study was on in vivo dual-responsive, tumor-specific imaging, encompassing both PAI and FLI. Then the in vivo dual-targeting ability of tumor-specific PAI and FLI was verified in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice. Three groups including FTNPs (without targeting ability), FTRNPs (only with active targeting by RGD peptides), and FTRNPs+MT (with dual targeting abilities) (Cy5.5-labeled on the nanoplatforms when FLI) were identified. It was found that both the FTRNPs and FTRNPs+MT groups peaked in fluorescence intensity 24 hours after systemic administration, while the FTNPs group consistently showed lower fluorescence (Fig. 11Bi). 3D in vivo PAI by PAMe confirmed these findings (Fig. 11Biv, C). Tumor fluorescence in major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney) indicated FTRNPs' renal elimination, partly involving the liver (Fig. 11Bii, Biii). The in vivo PTI and PTT of FTRNP group were further assessed. Three groups: FTRNPs, FTRNPs+MT, and a PBS control. At 24 hours post-injection, mice received an 808 nm NIR laser (Fig. 11Di). Tumor temperatures in FTRNPs+MT and FTRNPs groups peaked at 61.7℃ and 45.3℃, respectively, while the control group showed minimal change. These results indicated the high efficiency of FTRNPs with necessity of the dual targeting mechanisms. In summary, Chen et al. constructed a typical example of nanozyme-based theranostic nanoplatform, with dual targeting, H2O2, acidity-dual responsiveness, dual-treatment approaches, and three-mode real-time imaging approaches for both cancer therapy and therapeutic monitoring. However, a limitation of this study is the lack of orthotopic transplantation tumor experiments. This is also a common limitation of many studies on this topic. Besides, construction of nanozymes with multi-enzyme-mimicking functions or inclusion of other enzyme-mimicking substance such as GOx to keep H2O2 level and acidity in TME may enhance imaging performance and antitumor effects with a prolonged response time [63, 212–215].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of nanozymes in cancer treatment

ce:section.ce:para: Before discussing applications of nanozymes in cancer treatment, some common and useful treatment strategies of nanozymes should be summarized, which are vital for the design and development of nanozymes. After comprehensively reviewing recent publications of this field, we classify nanozyme-based treatment strategies into: I. Catalytic activity-based strategies: 1) ROS generation, 2) GSH depletion, 3) Starvation therapy, 4) Oxygen supply, 5) TME remodeling and Immunogenic cell death (ICD); II. Nanostructure-based non-catalytic strategies: 1) Drug delivery, 2) Photodynamic and sonodynamic Effects, 3) Photothermal and magnetic hyperthermia effects, 4) Prodrug Activation, 5) Ion imbalance ( Fig. 12). It is worth noting that different strategies can be largely related to each other. Based on the empirical conditions, selecting nanozymes and treatment approaches and combining feasible strategies is vital to maximize antitumor effects.
ROS generation

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are small highly reactive oxygen-containing chemical species [216]. In the scientometric analysis, ROS was the first clustering keyword as well as a high frequency keyword, indicating its central role in this field. ROS mainly include free radicals such a •OH, O2 •− and nonradical molecules like H2O2, 1O2, etc. [216]. While ROS at physiologically appropriate levels can serve as redox messengers and contribute significantly to intracellular signaling and homeostatic regulation, the increased intracellular ROS levels are usually regarded harmful, which can induce abnormal activities such as oxidative stress, DNA damage and cell death. Nanozymes with some specific catalytic activities (such as OXD-like, POD-like activities) can promote the production of ROS. Among all ROS types, •OH has the strongest capacity of oxidation, and it is also the most common ROS to induce oxidative stress, and further cellular damages such as DNA damage and lipid oxidation in nanozyme-based cancer treatment [217]. Moreover, depending on nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms with multi-enzyme mimicking abilities, ROS levels can be greatly increased due to the cascade reactions [218]. Besides, iron (such as Fe3+) and many other metal elements such as copper, manganese, cobalt, titanium, vanadium, palladium, silver, molybdenum, ruthenium, tungsten, cerium, zinc can mediate Fenton or Fenton-like reactions to upregulate •OH level, hence, iron or other metal-based nanozymes have been widely used in cancer treatment [219]. These Fenton and Fenton-like reaction-mediated cancer therapies are called chemodynamic therapy (CDT), which takes advantage of endogenous H2O2 in the TME to generate in situ •OH for killing cancer cells [220,221]. CDT can be segmented into two main components: one involves the chemical processes encompassing all reactions linked to the generation of •OH, while the other encompasses the biological processes, which encompass all intracellular biochemical reactions and subsequent cellular responses triggered by the presence of •OH [220]. Moreover, it is important to highlight that the differences and connections between CDT and catalytic therapy. CDT are usually mediated by nanomaterials containing iron and other transition metals, therefore, both nanozymes and other types of nanomaterials can trigger the process of CDT [222]. Besides, owing to the fast development of POD-like nanozymes in the field of cancer treatment, some studies also categorized POD-mediated •OH increase into the scope of CDT [223,224]. Catalytic therapy is a wide conception which mainly refers to nano-based catalytic therapy in this review. And this review holds the idea that all nanozyme-based therapies utilizing their catalytic activities to mediate or enhance antitumor effects can be categorized into catalytic therapies [7]. But on the contrary, nano-based catalytic therapy is not always dependent on nanozymes, since some other types of nanomaterials (e.g. composite nanomaterials carrying certain enzymes) can also mediate catalytic therapy [222,225]. Overall, CDT is either mediated by Fenton/Fenton-like reactions or medicated by POD-like activity, and it is usually considered as a type of catalytic therapy.

Other ROS types such as H2O2, O2 •−, 1O2 are also closely associated with oxidative stress and cell damage. Moreover, their roles as the substrate being catalyzed by nanozyme-based nanoplatform are also important in the field of cancer treatment. H2O2 is the most abundant ROS in the TME, playing multiple roles in various cellular activities [217,226]. Characterized as relatively less toxic and high level in the TME, H2O2 is one of the most important substrates in catalytic therapies based on the POD-like activity or Fenton/Fenton-like reactions of nanozymes. H2O2 can be classified into endogenous H2O2 and exogenous H2O2. It is widely acknowledged that the TME is characterized as overexpressed H2O2, which is the precondition for specific nanozymes to produce more •OH with higher toxicity. However, H2O2 level in the TME is still often considered to be insufficient when designing specific nanozymes for tumor CDT. Therefore, several approaches have been explored and applied to increase H2O2 level in the TME. The most common and important approach is to catalyze the production of endogenous H2O2, depending on OXD-like nanozymes or OXD-loaded nanoplatforms. Among several OXDs used in this field such as GOx, lactate oxidase, NADH oxidase, cholesterol oxidase, cytochrome c oxidase, GOx is the most common and useful one, because of its two roles including H2O2 production and glucose depletion (i.e., starvation therapy) [227,228]. The in situ production of H2O2 in the TME through this way can also prevent the potential toxicity to the surrounding normal tissues. Another approach is to introduce endogenous H2O2 into the TME, which is mainly based on the encapsulation and delivery of nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms [229–232]. Thus, the increased level of H2O2 can promote POD-like activity or Fenton/Fenton-like reaction of nanozymes to produce more highly toxic •OH. Besides, CAT is a vital enzyme that can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into water and oxygen, and the increased level of H2O2 can also promote CAT-like activity of nanozymes to induce more oxygen supply. O2 •− also plays a crucial role in various biological processes including harmful oxidative stress within cells [125]. In normal cellular environment, superoxide is part of signaling molecules and immune responses, and is produced by certain enzymes such as NADPH oxidase [233,234]. However, when superoxide anions are produced in excess, they can contribute to harmful oxidative stress, leading to cell damage. Moreover, SOD, an enzyme that catalyzes the dismutation of O2 •− into oxygen and H2O2, helps to mitigate potential damage. SOD-like nanozymes are also widely used to enhance oxygen supply in cancer treatment [235]. Different from other ROS, singlet oxygen (1O2) is relatively specific for two dynamic treatment methods, sonophotodynamic therapy (SDT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [236–238]. In the type II PDT, 1O2 is produced through the direct excitation energy transfer between the excited triplet state of the photosensitizers and O2
[239, 240]. The principle of 1O2 production in SDT is similar to the type II PDT, with an exception for the method used to activate the sensitizer [241,242].

Besides increasing the production of ROS, blocking the transcellular channels that mediate ROS efflux may also become an effective way to improve ROS accumulation [243,244]. Recently, Li et al. [245] designed a multifunctional nanozyme (FePGOGA) that co-loaded GOx and GAP19 peptides to mediate triple mode of cancer treatment approaches (catalytic therapy, starvation therapy, and photothermal therapy). In this investigation, GAP19 peptides effectively obstructed hemichannels by instigating the degradation of Cx43, consequently leading to an elevated accumulation of intracellular ROS.

Induction of Reactive nitrogen species (RNS) is also used as a strategy for nanozyme-based cancer therapy [246]. It is worth noting that some commonly described strategies in many studies, such as lipoxygenase, DNA damage, ferroptosis, apoptosis, necrosis, can be attributed and categorized to ROS increase, based on their actual principles [71,247,248]. Nevertheless, decreasing and scavenging ROS or RNS is also used in some studies related to nanozyme-based cancer therapy, which are mainly associated with inflammation-related cancer (e.g., colitis-associated colorectal cancer) [249–251].
GSH Depletion

Glutathione (GSH) is a tripeptide molecule (composed of glutamate, cysteine, and glycine) and plays a vital role in protecting cells from oxidative stress [252]. In its reduced form GSH, it serves as an essential antioxidant in normal cells, preventing damage to important cellular components caused by ROS [253]. Therefore, GSH depletion is regarded as a useful and powerful strategy to enhance therapeutic effects of ROS-based cancer therapies [254]. In 2021, Niu et al. [248] summarized the recent strategies of GSH depletion for cancer therapy, which can be divided into four categories based on different mechanisms: 1) consuming GSH reserves 2) cutting off the supply of ingredients for GSH synthesis; 3) inhibiting GSH synthesis or regeneration; 4) promoting GSH efflux.

Consuming GSH reserves is the most common strategy for GSH depletion. For nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms with GSHOx- or GPx-like activities or other oxidizing substance, GSH can be oxidized into GSSG, thus achieving the depletion of GSH [255]. Besides, disulfide bonds (S-S) contained in the nanoplatforms can also react with GSH and then transfer to sulfhydryl group [256]. The other three strategies for GSH depletion are mainly associated with inhibitors of certain proteins or enzymes. Thus, these three strategies-based applications of nanozymes mainly depend on their abilities of drug delivery. First, cutting off the ingredients for GSH synthesis can be mainly achieved by blocking the transcellular transport of specific amino acids (especially cystine/cysteine) [257]. Cysteine starvation by inhibiting system Xc - is a common approach for this mode [257]. Extracellular cystine enters cells through system Xc- and is then converted to cysteine (Cys) for protein and GSH synthesis. Targeting and inhibiting system Xc- can lead to Cys scarcity, causing intracellular GSH depletion. Common system Xc- inhibitors include erastin, imidazole ketone erastin (IKE), sulfasalazine, and sorafenib [257–259]. Additionally, inhibiting key enzymes and molecules involved in GSH synthesis and regeneration is an effective approach. The enzyme GCL, which limits GSH synthesis, can be targeted with its inhibitor, l-buthionine sulfoximine (BSO), to deplete GSH and enhance ROS-based therapies [260,261]. GSH regeneration is crucial and depends on the enzyme GR, which uses NADPH [262]. Therefore, depleting NADPH by either inhibiting the production of NADPH or consuming the produced NADPH is also a feasible way to achieve GSH depletion [248]. Third, extruding intracellular GSH out of target cells can also induce GSH depletion. GSH is supposed to firstly conjugated with specific molecules based on GSH detoxification, and then the resultant GS-X conjugates will be extruded out of the cells [248]. Therefore, exploring effective molecules for GSH conjugation is an important step for this mode of GSH depletion. Besides, MRP1, a member of the ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporter family, mediates the process of transcellular transport of GSH or GS-X conjugates [263]. Enhancing the activity of MRP1 may be also a way to enhance GSH efflux, though it is not commonly used in the current field.
Starvation Therapy

Starvation therapy refers to a therapeutic approach that aims to deprive cancer cells of nutrients and oxygen they need to grow and proliferate [264–268]. It is acknowledged that cancer cells often have increased metabolic needs compared to normal cells, and they may be more sensitive to restrictions in certain nutrients [265,267]. Thus, blocking the supply of these nutrients or oxygen for cancer cells is a feasible way to enhance antitumor effects. Starvation therapies can be categorized: 1) deprivation of nutrients and oxygen; 2) inhibition of tumor vasculature such as angiogenesis inhibition, vascular disruption, transarterial embolization; 3) dysfunction of cellular metabolism [213,264,269,270]. Since starvation therapy is usually combined with other standard therapies and plays an assistant role in nanozyme-based cancer treatment, we prefer to categorize starvation therapy into cancer treatment strategies [131, 213, 270–273].

For nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms, catalysis-mediated nutrient deprivation is the main strategy for starvation therapy.Of note, glucose depletion by GOx or GOx-like nanozymes is most used in this field [272, 274–276]. Moreover, most of these nanozymes are designed to be nanozyme-based nanoplatforms with the ability of cascade catalysis reactions. For example, GOx-loaded POD-like nanozymes can not only catalyze glucose into glucose acid, but also provide H2O2 to enhance the POD-like activity of nanozymes to produce more •OH, thus achieving the combined therapies [215,272]. Besides GOx, several other OXDs can also induce the deprivation of specific nutrients and/or dysfunction of cellular metabolism, such as lactate oxidase [138], NADH oxidase [139,277], cholesterol oxidase [140], cytochrome c oxidase [141]. Besides the deprivation of other nutrients such as amino acids, lipid is also studied in this field, which may be more dependable on specific drugs, molecules, or genes. In addition, cysteine starvation, which was introduced as a way of GSH depletion, can also be regarded as a way of nutrient deprivation.

Different from nutrient deprivation, oxygen deprivation or consumption seems to be more complicated in cancer therapy. As introduced, hypoxia is a disadvantage factor for most cancer therapies, and oxygen supply in the TME has been widely used in our discussed field. Nevertheless, oxygen deprivation in this review refers to the complete blockade or consumption of oxygen in the TME. However, this approach is usually difficult to be achieved with ideal results due to the complexity of the TME and the immaturity of current technology. Combing oxygen deprivation in the TME and tumor vasculature targeting strategy may be a more possible way to achieve [268]. Besides, inhibition of tumor vasculature and dysfunction of cellular metabolism by using nanoplatforms have also been studied [213,278,279], though it is not a common approach for nanozyme-based cancer therapy.

These three strategies are primarily mediated by increasing ROS, involving nanozymes with POD and OXD activities to promote the production of ROS such as •OH, H2O2, and O2 •−, leading to oxidative stress and cell damage, or consumption of nutrients. Additionally, iron and other metal-based nanozymes facilitate Fenton or Fenton-like reactions to elevate •OH levels, effectively killing cancer cells.
Oxygen Supply

In a manner akin to ROS, oxygen emerged as both a clustering keyword and a high-frequency keyword in the scientometric analysis. Furthermore, hypoxia was identified as a high-frequency keyword and a recent topic of significant interest, as indicated by its burst in keyword occurrence.

These results showed the importance of oxygen in nanozyme-based cancer therapy. It is widely acknowledged that hypoxia is a key factor in the progression of solid tumors. And the hypoxic TME is also regarded as a vital cause for resistance and insensitivity of cancer therapies. Within the hypoxic tumor microenvironment (TME), cancer cells undergo adaptive genetic alterations aimed at evading hypoxia-induced cell death and bolstering their survival. The orchestration of this process is chiefly governed by a family of heterodimeric transcription factors known as hypoxia-inducible factors (HIFs) [280,281]. Among these factors, HIF-1α is a prominent member, continually expressed by cells but subject to oxygen-dependent degradation. Under normal oxygen conditions, the prolyl hydroxylase domain protein (PHD) exerts its influence on HIF-1α by hydroxylating specific proline residues. This hydroxylation event fosters the binding of von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) protein to HIF-1α, subsequently initiating its ubiquitination and eventual proteasomal degradation. However, in the presence of hypoxic conditions, the PHD-mediated hydroxylation of HIF-1α is significantly impeded. This inhibition prevents the degradation of HIF-1α, leading to its accumulation within the cells. Consequently, cancer cells experiencing hypoxia-driven upregulation of HIF-1α initiate the expression of specific genes associated with various aspects of tumor survival, proliferation, metabolism, angiogenesis, metastasis, drug resistance, recurrence, and more [282,283]. Besides, the extent of hypoxia varies a lot with different positions within tumors.

Based on the huge adverse effects of hypoxia, oxygen supply to modulate the hypoxia TME has been a common and useful strategy for almost all cancer therapies [284–288]. Oxygen supply mainly depends on CAT- or SOD-like activities of nanozymes. Improving the hypoxia TME can not only inhibit hypoxia-induced tumor progression, but also promote the therapeutic effects of some oxygen-dependent therapies including radiotherapy (RT), photodynamic therapy (PDT), and sonodynamic therapy (SDT). In these oxygen-dependent therapies, oxygen acts as substrates or necessary substances to play the antitumor effects [289,290].

Besides oxygen supply, there are several other strategies used for countering or exploiting the hypoxia TME, such as targeting HIF-1α and other hypoxia-induced genes for therapeutic sensibilization [291,292], hypoxia-activated prodrugs [141, 293–296], etc.

For cancer treatment strategies, ROS generation-mediated oxidative stress and induced death of cancer cells are the most important strategies. This has been well established in solid tumors, but ROS scavenging is also a key strategy in hematological tumors such as leukemia [297–299]. The reason of this difference is related to the different occurrence and development mechanism of solid tumor and hematologic tumor and the difference of tumor microenvironment [300,301]. Since this area still needs further exploration and clarity, this article will not go into detail.
TME remodeling and ICD

In the scientometric analysis, "TME" was not only a high-frequency keyword but also displayed the most substantial citation burst, underscoring its growing relevance and importance in the field. With the deeper understanding of TME and continuous development of immunotherapies such as immune checkpoint blockades and certain targeted therapies such as bevacizumab (an anti-angiogenic agent), TME remodeling has been a vital strategy to enhance antitumor effects [302]. TME remodeling primarily involves altering the tumor's physiological environment, including factors such as pH, oxygen levels, and the presence of immunosuppressive cells. The objective is to transform the immunosuppressive TME into an immune-activated TME. Moreover, different components in TME can interact with each other to enhance the immunosuppressive TME. For example, studies showed that low pH or acid environment contributes to dysfunction of T and NK cells, as well as maintaining of myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) and regulatory T cells (Tregs) [303]. Therefore, targeting dual or multiple components in the TME may be a promising strategy to play stronger antitumor effects. In this review, we categorize nanozyme-based TME remodeling into direct and indirect approaches.

For nanozyme-based cancer treatment, oxygen supply based on CAT- or SOD-like activities is regarded as the main direct approach to remodel the TME. It is widely acknowledged that hypoxia can directly enhance the immunosuppression of MDSCs, Tregs, tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) (promotion and maintenance of M2 type polarization) [304–307]. Moreover, recent studies also discovered that hypoxia can influence the crosstalk and communication between tumor cells and these immunosuppressive cells [304]. These studies lay the foundation for oxygen supply-based catalytic therapy in this field. In 2022, Phan et al. categorized strategies for nanozyme- enhanced cancer immunotherapy into ROS-generating nanozymes for inducing ICD, and ROS-scavenging nanozymes for oxygen generation and further TME remodeling [308]. However, it should be noted that many nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms are characteristic with multiple catalytic activities, determining that they may act as both ROS-scavenging nanozymes for oxygen production and ROS-generating nanozymes for oxidative stress and cell damages [309,310].

Immunogenic cell death (ICD) denotes a type of cellular demise that elicits an immune response directed at antigens from the deceased cells [311,312]. During the process of ICD, dying cells release specific molecules known as damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs). Simultaneously, they display tumor-associated antigens (TAAs) in a manner that prompts the maturation of dendritic cells (DCs). Consequently, DCs present these antigens to T cells, leading to their activation into cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs). This activation also augments the antitumor effects of natural killer (NK) cells. As a result, ICD possesses the capacity to stimulate both innate and adaptive immune responses, thus fostering robust antitumor immune reactions. Furthermore, it's worth noting that ICD can be triggered by a range of cancer therapies, including immunotherapy, chemotherapy, PTT, PDT, SDT, and more [313–317]. For nanozyme-based cancer therapies, POD- or OXD-like nanozymes can induce oxidative stress and subsequent ICD by promoting the generation of ROS, such as •OH, H2O2, or O2 •- [308,318]. Besides, nanozyme-based PDT and SDT can also promote 1O2 generation to induce ICD [315,317].

In addition to strategies based on the catalytic activities of nanozymes, another aspect that cannot be overlooked is the physicochemical properties of nanozymes themselves. They are not limited to nanozymes but also exist in other nanomaterials. Since their function has significant implications for not only catalytic activities but also subsequent antitumor strategies, it is necessary to briefly review these physicochemical aspects.
1)
Drug Delivery
In the scientometric analysis, drug delivery was a high-frequency keyword, indicating its wide applications in this field. Nanozymes are nanomaterials with enzyme-like characteristics, and many types of nanozymes with suitable nanostructures are capable of delivering drugs and therapeutic genes. Common examples include MOFs, Au NPs, F3O4 NPs, MnO2 NPs, carbon dots, etc. [48, 59, 213, 319–321]. Besides, nanozymes characterized with smaller sizes can also be encapsulated into nanocarriers with large loading spaces to form the composite nanoplatforms [322,323]. Moreover, similar to nano-based drug delivery systems, two key points of nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms should be stressed. One is the targeting ability, which can be obtained by specific modifications of nanocarriers [6,324]. And the other is the responsiveness, mainly refers to TME-responsiveness such as pH, hypoxia, GSH [205]. These two points enhance the targeting ability and controlled therapeutic effect of nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms in cancer treatment. Moreover, nanozyme-based drug delivery can largely promote the development of combined therapies of different modes, such as catalytic therapy plus chemotherapy. Besides, some photosensitizers and sonosensitizers of individual components used in the photodynamic therapy and sonodynamic therapy can also be regarded as applications of drug delivery.
2)
Photodynamic and Sonodynamic Effects
Photodynamic and sonodynamic effects are primarily utilized in PDT and SDT, respectively. PDT is a promising cancer treatment strategy that involves exposing specific photosensitizers to light of a particular wavelength to produce ROS, which induces oxidative damage to cancer cells and tissues [325]. In this context, photosensitizers can either be nanozymes themselves or traditional photosensitizers carried by nanozymes [326]. This light-activated mechanism allows for precise spatiotemporal control over cancer treatment, minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissues [327].
SDT, on the other hand, employs ultrasound to activate sonosensitizers, generating ROS to mediate antitumor effects [328]. Similar to PDT, sonosensitizers can be nanozymes themselves or sonosensitizers loaded onto nanozymes. Benefiting from the properties of sound waves, SDT offers deeper tissue penetration and less tissue damage, making it suitable for treating deep-seated tumors while causing minimal harm to healthy tissues [329].
3)
Photothermal and Magnetic Hyperthermia Effects
PTT and magnetic hyperthermia therapy (MHT) rely on the photothermal and magnetic-thermal conversion effects of photothermal agents and magnetic hyperthermia agents, respectively. These agents can be nanozymes themselves or specific conversion agents carried by nanozymes. In PTT, photothermal agents absorb light of a particular wavelength and convert it into heat, resulting in localized high temperatures that lead to cancer cell death [330]. This heat generation is localized and can be controlled by adjusting the intensity and duration of NIR light exposure. Similarly, in MHT, when magnetic hyperthermia agents are exposed to an alternating magnetic field, the heat generated can induce cancer cell death, exerting antitumor effects [331]. Additionally, due to the superior physicochemical properties of nanozymes, these methods also allow for real-time imaging and monitoring of treatment progress through thermal imaging or MRI, making them powerful tools in the fight against cancer [332,333].
4)
Prodrug Activation
Bioorthogonal reactions refer to chemical reactions that occur within living organisms or biologically relevant environments. They exhibit high specificity and non-toxicity, avoiding interference with other biochemical processes within the organism. In the field of nanotechnology, bioorthogonal reactions are widely utilized for constructing nanostructures, functionalizing nanomaterials, and controlling the properties and behaviors of nanostructures. These reactions enable surface modification of nanoparticles, design of drug carriers, detection, and localization of biomarkers, among other applications. Additionally, there are three modes of bioorthogonal reaction-based drug synthesis or prodrug activation, including uncaging [146], coupling, and intramolecular reactions. Nanozymes, as a special class of nanomaterials, possess biological catalytic activity and high specificity, making them suitable for mediating bioorthogonal reactions, thereby demonstrating significant potential in fields such as cancer treatment. Moreover, TMCs used in bioorthogonal nanozymes can also mimic other catalytic activities such as POD, thereby exerting multiple antitumor mechanisms and enhancing nanozyme-based therapeutic effects [334].
5)
Ion imbalance
Depending on the constituent nanomaterials, ion imbalance, such as the overload of Ca2+, Fe2+, and Na+, is another important aspect of nanozyme application in cancer therapy since it can significantly affect cellular processes and induce disturbance and death of cancer cells [335–337]. The ability to manipulate ion concentrations within cells or tissues allows for innovative therapeutic strategies that can disrupt cancer cell homeostasis and induce cytotoxic effects.

Currently, nanozyme-based cancer therapies have been a hot topic in both fields of chemistry and medical science. More and more scholars take part in nanozyme-related research, and actively explore its application in the field cancer treatment. Though various kinds of treatment approaches have been proposed in nanozyme-based cancer therapies, there is still a lack of a reliable and useful classification [11]. Thus, this review would like to sum up a classification for nanozyme-based cancer therapies, based on the comprehensive review on relevant studies in recent years.

Based on the definition of nanocatalytic medicine, this review holds the idea that all nanozyme-based therapies utilizing their catalytic activities to mediate or enhance antitumor effects can be categorized into catalytic therapies. Moreover, this review further categorizes nanozyme-based cancer treatment approaches into catalytic therapy induced/plus CDT, catalytic therapy induced/plus immunotherapy, catalytic therapy plus chemotherapy, catalytic therapy plus radiotherapy, catalytic therapy plus PTT or MHT), catalytic therapy plus PDT, and catalytic therapy plus SDT, based on the investigations and statistics of recent related studies ( Fig. 13 ,
Tables 5–12). Moreover, it was found most studies relevant to nanozyme-based cancer treatment are adopting combined or synergistic therapies instead of monotherapies. These insights are further supported by both cluster analysis and burst analysis of keywords.

CDT is an emerging cancer therapeutic approach firstly proposed by Shi group [342] in 2016. Our scientometric analysis found that CDT was not only a high frequency keyword but also a burst keyword in 2021, indicting its emerging status with wide applications in this field. CDT is now defined as cancer treatment exploiting Fenton or Fenton-like reactions to produce highly toxic •OH in the tumor region [220,221,342]. Fenton reaction refers to the generation of •OH from H2O2 under the catalysis of Fe2+. Then, research on Fenton or Fenton-like reactions has expanded to other catalysts, especially nanomaterials-based catalysts including iron and other metals (such as Cu, Mn, Co, Ti, V, Pd, Ag, Mo, Ru, Ce)-based nanomaterials, and organic nanomaterials [343]. Besides, the Fenton reaction was identified as a clustering keyword, representing its vital role in this field. It is widely acknowledged that POD-like activity of nanozymes is closely associated with Fenton or Fenton-like reactions [344–349]. Therefore, many studies and this review would like to incorporate POD-like nanozyme-mediated •OH production into the category of CDT, and catalytic therapy induced CDT is preferred for this condition. The utilization of nanozyme-based CDT is distinguished by its elevated efficacy, precise target specificity, minimal adverse effects, and limited equipment requisites. Compared with other ROS-mediated cancer therapies, such as PDT or SDT, CDT is a laser, ultrasound, sensitizers, and oxygen-independent therapy, thereby avoiding restrictions of penetration depth or hypoxic TME [220].

Our summary of recent studies on nanozyme-based catalytic therapy induced/plus CDT (Table 5) also indicated CDT or CDT-based combined therapies have been a hot topic in recent years. Almost all nanozymes are characterized with POD-like activity with or without other activities. Several common treatment strategies including ROS increase, GSH depletion, oxygen supply, glucose depletion, and drug delivery, were identified from Table 5. Among various treatment approaches, CDT, catalytic therapy plus CDT, and CDT plus PTT are the most common ones. Moreover, it can also be indicated that combined therapies containing CDT are probably the mainstream of future development for this field. However, it was found that few studies incorporated nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms with targeting abilities.

In 2023, Liu et al. [139] presented a notable illustration of nanozyme-mediated catalytic therapy combined with CDT. In the investigation, the authors engineered an innovative single-atom nanozyme termed Ir-N5 SA. This specific nanozyme exhibited an asymmetric electron distribution, a characteristic realized through the incorporation of melamine during the pyrolytic process, as illustrated in Fig. 14Ai. This distinctive attribute confers upon it the ability to surmount the constraints of suboptimal adsorption capacity and catalytic functionality commonly encountered in conventional metal-N4 coordination complexes with symmetric electron distributions [46,362]. Ir-N5 SA are characterized with enhanced multi-enzyme-mimicking abilities including OXD-, POD-, CAT-, and NAHD oxidase (NOX)-like activities (Fig. 14Aii). Then these catalytic activities form a typical cascade reaction to enhance the antitumor effect of nanozymes. Initially, Ir-N5 SA demonstrates the capacity to efficiently catalyze H2O2 within the TME, thereby generating oxygen through its CAT-like activity. This process serves to mitigate the hypoxic conditions commonly prevalent in tumor sites and simultaneously furnishes an oxygen supply requisite for its NOX-like activity. Then Ir-N5 SA can catalyze NADH to generate H2O2 for POD-like activity-mediated CDT. And the decreased NADH level inhibits normal mitochondrial function and glycolysis, which reduces the endogenous O2 consumption and enhances the antitumor effect. Moreover, the increased O2 and H2O2 levels enhanced OXD- and POD-like activities of Ir-N5 SA, respectively, inducing more ROS production and stronger oxidative stress. Besides, cerulenin (Cer) was loaded into Ir-N5 SA to further inhibit the fatty acid oxidation (FAO) metabolism of tumor cells experiencing metabolic reprogramming in nutrient-deficient TME. After preliminary verification in the in vitro studies, the in vivo antitumor effect Ir-N5 SA was further evaluated in breast cancer (4T1 cells)-bearing mice. Among four groups including Ir-N4 SA, Ir-N5 SA, and Ir-N5 SA/Cer groups, Ir-N5 SA and Ir-N5 SA/Cer groups exhibited tumor inhibition rates of 58.7 % and 78.5 %, respectively, much higher than Ir-N4 SA group (Fig. 14Bv). Moreover, survival time in mice treated with Ir-N5 SA and Ir-N5 SA/Cer was significantly extended (Fig. 14Bvii), with side effects similar to control and Ir-N4 SA groups (Fig. 14Bviii, Bix). Overall, this work revealed a typical example of nanozymes-mediated catalytic therapy induced/plus CDT. The four types of catalytic activities of nanozymes forms an efficient cascade reaction to overcome the insufficiency of endogenous H2O2 and enhance the antitumor effect. Moreover, dual inhibitions of glucose metabolism and FAO metabolism inhibit ATP production, resulting in the starvation therapy and enhanced antitumor effect of the nanozyme.

Recently, Liang et al. [359] proposed a RhRu alloy-anchored transition metal carbide (MXene) nanozyme-based nanoplatform (RhRu/Ti3C2T x ) for osteosarcoma therapy. RhRu nanozyme was grown on 2D Ti3C2T x nanosheets, thus generating excellent electron interactions for enhancing catalytic activity of the nanozyme. RhRu/Ti3C2T x simultaneously owns four intrinsic properties including POD-like activity, CAT-like activity, photothermal performance, and photodynamic performance. These properties mainly originate from RhRu nanozyme, whereas Ti3C2T x carrier also represents good photothermal performance [363]. Moreover, these properties of RhRu/Ti3C2T x exhibit excellent synergistic antitumor effects in the treatment of osteosarcoma. Upon exposure to 808 nm laser irradiation, the nanoplatform exhibited augmented POD-like activity, thereby facilitating an elevated generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) to potentiate CDT. The photodynamic performance of RhRu/Ti3C2T x was also enhanced by O2 supply because of its intrinsic CAT-like activity. Based on excellent properties of the nanozyme-based nanoplatform with reliable in vitro studies, both subcutaneous and orthotopic osteosarcoma models (143B cells) were constructed to verify the in vivo antitumor effects ( Fig. 15Bi). Initially, the photothermal capabilities of the RhRu/ Ti3C2T x nanoplatform were validated in vivo following intratumoral administration of either an aqueous RhRu/ Ti3C2T x solution or normal saline, and subsequent exposure to 808 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 15Bii). Among four intervention groups including control, Ti3C2T x , RhRu/Ti3C2T x , and RhRu/Ti3C2T x +NIR groups in both subcutaneous (Fig. 15Biv, Bv) and orthotopic (Fig. 15Bxi, Bix) osteosarcoma models, tumor volume changes showed the strongest antitumor effect was induced by RhRu/Ti3C2T x +NIR group through nanozyme-based synergistic CDT/PDT/PTT. And similarly limited side effects were observed in each group (Fig. 15Biii, Bx). Additionally, histological evaluations using H&E, TUNEL/DAPI, and HIF-1α staining assays on tumor slices from each group revealed multiple potential mechanisms for the observed antitumor effects. These include necrosis, apoptosis, in situ oxygen supply, and inhibition of cell proliferation (Fig. 15Bvi-ix). Overall, this study designed a novel multimode treatment (CDT/PDT/PTT)-based nanozyme-based nanoplatform for effective osteosarcoma treatment, with the independence of other drugs or reagents such as photosensitizers. Moreover, including orthotopic transplantation tumor experiment provides evidence for RhRu/Ti3C2T x for effective osteosarcoma treatment. Nevertheless, it may be difficult or have some adverse effects (e.g., infection) for intratumoral injection of RhRu/Ti3C2T x in the practical osteosarcoma treatment, since osteosarcoma often occurs deep in the bone. Therefore, appropriate modifications of the RhRu/Ti3C2T x to enhance its targeting ability and biocompatibility for potential intravenous administration may be a choice.

Immunotherapy serves as a therapeutic modality in oncology designed to mobilize the inherent capabilities of the patient's immune system for the identification and eradication of malignant cells. This approach aims to amplify or reconstitute immunological functions to achieve targeted, often durable, antitumor effects [364]. In contrast to traditional therapies like chemotherapy and radiotherapy, immunotherapy enhances the body's immune responses or mitigates immunosuppression in the TME [365,366]. Moreover, corresponding to the overall mechanisms, the strategies of nanozyme-mediated immunotherapy mainly include ROS increase-mediated ICD and later immune response and O2 supply-mediated TME remodeling.

In 2022, Zhao et al. [370] designed a 4T1 cell membranes-coated multienzyme-mimic manganese oxide (MnOx) nanozyme called CM@Mn for catalytic immunotherapy, which clarifies the basic mechanisms of this treatment approach. CM@Mn was featured as three mimicking catalytic activities including OXD-, POD-, and CAT-like activities ( Fig. 16A). The mechanisms of CM@Mn plus anti-PD-1 therapy for cancer treatment can be described as follows. First, the OXD- and POD-like activities of CM@Mn promote the production of ROS (•OH and O2 •-), which then induces oxidative stress and ICD of cancer cells. Second, the Mn2+ liberated from MnOx function as agonists for the cyclic GMP-AMP synthase/stimulator of interferon genes pathway. This activation fosters dendritic cell maturation and induces a shift in macrophage polarization towards the M1 phenotype, thereby amplifying immune responses and reshaping the TME. Furthermore, this M1 macrophage polarization has been shown to elevate intratumoral H2O2 levels, which may synergistically enhance the POD- and CAT-like activities of CM@Mn nanozymes [309,379]. Third, combining with PD-1 blockade further strengthens the antitumor immune responses. Finally, the CAT-like activity of CM@Mn also helps relieve hypoxia, which can not only enhance OXD-like activity but also promote TME remodeling. After verifying the catalytic activities and antitumor effects of CM@Mn with certain mechanisms at the cellular level, Zhao et al. then conducted in vivo studies to prove its targeting ability and therapeutic effects in 4T1 primary and second tumor mouse models. There were six groups, including saline, MnCl2, αPD-1, CM@Mn, MnCl2+αPD-1, and CM@Mn+αPD-1. The study revealed significant increases in the M1/M2 macrophage ratio and the maturation rate of DCs in the CM@Mn+αPD-1 group, as shown in Fig. 16Biii. This group also saw elevated proportions of CD8+CTLs and CD4+ T cells, reaching 24.98 % and 37.38 %, respectively (P<0.01). Additionally, higher levels of TNF-α, IL-12p70, and IFN-γ were observed in both tumors and serum in the CM@Mn+αPD-1 group, as indicated in Fig. 16Bvi. Additionally, the CM@Mn+αPD-1 group exhibited a substantial rise in the proportions of Tem and Tcm, affirming the immune memory benefits of combining CM@Mn with PD-1 blockade (Fig. 16Bviii). Lastly, the CM@Mn+αPD-1 group demonstrated the most potent tumor-suppressive effects among all treatment cohorts, as delineated in Fig. 16Bii-v. This group also significantly extended the survival duration of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice, as evidenced in Fig. 16Bvii. Moreover, effects of eliminating metastatic tumors of different intervention groups were explored. CM@Mn+αPD-1 administration was proved to provide the strongest tumor-suppressive effect and longest survival among all groups. Overall, this study revealed the two basic strategies for catalytic immunotherapy, with the verification of in vivo antitumor effects of combined catalytic therapy and ICB.

As insights into the TME and its role in cancer progression deepen, strategies targeting other immunosuppressive elements within the TME, such as macrophages and MDSCs, have gained feasibility [249,373,380]. Considering actual conditions of nanozymes’ designing and fabrication, pursuing and exploring novel nanozymes with different but feasible antitumor mechanisms in both cancer cells and other components in the TME is probably the future direction.

As a high frequency keyword, chemotherapy has been widely used in combination with catalytic therapy to combat with cancers. Moreover, since chemotherapy currently acts as the first-line therapy in the treatment of most cancers, there is big potential for the future success of catalytic therapy plus chemotherapy.

After reviewing recent studies of this field (Table 7), it was found that most studies were proposed after 2021. Besides common catalytic activities such as POD-, CAT-, OXD-, SOD-like activities, bioorthogonal catalytic activity has also been studied in recent years. Accordingly, there are also different kinds of treatment strategies in catalytic therapy induced/plus immunotherapies, such as ROS increase, GSH depletion, oxygen supply, drug delivery, and prodrug activation. Particularly, the specificity of drug delivery and prodrug activation in this therapeutic modality aligns well with the current advancements in nano-based drug delivery systems and bioorthogonal reaction-based therapies [324, 381–383]. Moreover, it should be known that the main goals of catalytic therapy plus chemotherapy are to reduce or eliminate drug resistance, enhance therapeutic effects, and decrease or avoid systemic toxicity. There are some common drugs used in chemotherapy plus catalytic therapy, including 5-fluorouracil (5FU), Doxorubicin (DOX), Paclitaxel (PTX), Mitoxantrone, etc. They can either be loaded or conjugated with nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms, or be conventionally administered. And it is worth noting that combination therapy should not simply combine two different treatment modes or drugs, instead, the detailed combination therapy-enhanced antitumor mechanism should be verified. Therefore, deepening the understanding of mechanisms of existing and novel chemotherapeutic drugs and exploring how to maximize the antitumor effects of chemotherapy with the combination of catalytic therapy, is likely to be the developmental trend.

In 2022, Li et al. proposed an interesting nuclease-mimetic Pt nanozymes (NMPNs) for concurrent DNA platination and oxidative cleavage [73]. Initially, NMPNs were engineered with surface modifications: mPEG5K-AC-CA for sensitivity to the acidic TME, and TAT peptides to augment endocytosis, ensure endosomal escape, and facilitate nuclear entry. Upon nuclear localization, as depicted in Fig. 17J, NMPNs efficiently liberate Pt ions. These ions subsequently form Pt-DNA adducts, culminating in DNA damage and the induction of apoptosis in cisplatin-resistant tumor cells. Additionally, NMPNs display OXD- and POD-like catalytic activities, engendering in situ ROS generation. This contributes to oxidative DNA cleavage proximal to the Pt-DNA binding sites, thereby augmenting the therapeutic efficacy. This, in turn, disrupts DNA conformation and interferes with nucleotide excision repair (NER). Moreover, NMPNs interfere with the recruitment of NER effectors, notably xeroderma pigmentosum groups A (XPA) and F (XPF), to DNA lesion sites, as illustrated in Fig. 17F, G. This disruption further amplifies the therapeutic impact. Ultimately, NMPNs have been empirically shown to trigger apoptosis in cisplatin-resistant tumor cells, manifesting robust anti-tumor activity in an orthotopic liver tumor mouse model (Fig. 17B, C, D, E, I). Overall, this work provides a good example for nanozyme-based catalytic therapy combined with chemotherapy, which helps combat with drug resistance and enhance antitumor effects. Moreover, as the authors suggested, NMPNs may also represent the next generation Pt-based anticancer agents due to the easy modification for targeting ability and responsiveness and catalytic activities-enhanced antitumor effects [73].

Indeed, radiotherapy is a fundamental component of cancer treatment, often used in conjunction with surgery and chemotherapy. Radiotherapy encompasses the use of various forms of radiation, including ionizing radiation such as X-rays and γ-rays, as well as particle radiation, which includes α-rays and β-rays. These radiation modalities are employed to target and destroy cancer cells or inhibit their growth, contributing to the overall therapeutic approach to manage and combat cancer [392]. The antitumor mechanisms of radiotherapy can be classified into direct mechanism and indirect mechanism. Take X-rays as an example. X-rays act in two main ways: they directly damage cellular DNA or interact with water to produce high levels of ROS, which then harm cellular components. However, traditional radiotherapy faces several problems including high radiation doses with severe side effects for healthy tissues around tumors, tumor hypoxia resulting in weak therapeutic effect and resistance, etc. [286,290,393]. Therefore, overcoming these challenges and improving therapeutic effect is the continuingly pursuing goal of radiotherapy [148].

In recent years, nanozyme-based catalytic therapy plus radiotherapy has been a feasible way to overcome the common challenges and problems. Table 8 summarizes recent typical applications of nanozyme-based catalytic therapy plus radiotherapy. Several common treatment modes are identified, including Radiotherapy plus Catalytic therapy, and Radiotherapy plus Catalytic therapy and Chemotherapy/ Gene therapy/ Immunotherapy/Radiodynamic therapy. It is worth noting that studies of nanozymes for enhancing radiotherapy are relatively less than that of nanozymes for CDT/immunotherapy/chemotherapy. Another difference is that CAT becomes a key catalytic activity of nanozymes in this mode, because of its ability of oxygen supply to improve the hypoxia TME and induce stronger antitumor effect of radiotherapy [394]. Specially, nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms used in radiotherapy are usually constructed by high-Z elements (e.g., Pb, Pt, Au, Ag, Ru) with high radiation absorption coefficient, which can play roles of radiosensitizing and synergistic therapeutic effects [395]. Besides, it can also reduce radiation doses and alleviate side effects of radiotherapy.

Hu et al. [54] reported a typical example of nanozyme for catalytic therapy plus radiotherapy. In this work, PEGylated RuCu nanoparticles (P-RuCu) was designed as the nanozyme to serve as the radiosensitizer. P-RuCu exhibits CAT- and POD-like activities. It reacts with elevated H2O2 in the TME to generate O2, alleviating tumor hypoxia, and produces toxic •OH to enhance antitumor effects ( Fig. 18A). Additionally, the high-Z element Ru in P-RuCu enhances X-ray radiation absorption in tumor cells by depositing greater energy [395]. An in vitro study in MDA-MB-231 cells demonstrated that P-RuCu was absorbed into cells through endocytosis. Based on the POD- and CAT-like activities, P-RuCu promoted the generation of high-level ROS and alleviated tumor hypoxia, with a large influence on the expression of HIF-1α. Histone H2AX serves as a highly sensitive indicator for quantifying radiation-induced damage in cells, as it undergoes rapid phosphorylation to generate γ-H2AX foci. Notably, when subjected to X-ray treatment in conjunction with P-RuCu, the number of γ-H2AX foci exhibited the most significant increase within just one hour of irradiation (Fig. 18Bi, Biii). Moreover, the sensitizer enhancement ratio of P-RuCu when combined with X-ray treatment was found to be 1.31 in MDA-MB-231 cells (Fig. 18Bii). This study further verified the radiosensitizer role of P-RuCu. Among four groups including control (saline), P-RuCu, X-ray, and P-RuCu plus X-ray groups, P-RuCu plus X-ray treatment remarkably enhanced tumor growth inhibition and therapeutic efficacy (Fig. 18Biv-vi), while exhibited similar toxicity to other groups. In summary, this work represents a typical model for nanozyme-based catalytic therapy plus radiotherapy. Nevertheless, it was found that the duration of efficient γ-H2AX foci increase for P-RuCu plus X-ray treatment was less than 6 h due to the cellular repair capability (Fig. 18Bi, Bii). Therefore, combining P-RuCu plus X-ray treatment with targeting specific DNA repair systems may be a more powerful method [73].

Hyperthermia therapy serves as an adjuvant modality in oncological care, utilizing elevated temperature as a therapeutic mechanism [399]. Though further and deeper understanding of hyperthermia therapy is needed, its main mechanism can be simply described as two aspects. Heat in hyperthermia therapy induces multiple cellular responses, including protein denaturation, heat-shock protein synthesis, metabolic dysfunction, and DNA damage, culminating in cell death [400–402]. Notably, cancer cells exhibit increased thermal sensitivity within specific temperature ranges compared to normal cells. And a range of 41-48 °C is usually considered for hyperthermia therapy in cancer treatment [400,403,404].

There are many approaches for heat production in hyperthermia therapy, but laser-activated PTT and alternating magnetic field (AMF)-activated MHT are the two mainstream approaches in nano-based hyperthermia therapy [400,403,405]. Tables 9 and 10 summarized recent applications of nanozyme-based catalytic therapy plus PTT and MHT, respectively. In PTT, a laser irradiates the tumor regions, and photothermal conversion agents efficiently convert optical energy into heat [406,407]. PTT provides remarkable selectivity via fine-tuned laser parameters including power density, irradiation duration, and optical wavelength [408,409]. The near-infrared (NIR) biological window, particularly the 700–1400 nm range, has attracted significant research attention. This is primarily because it allows for deep tissue penetration while minimizing self-heating of the tissue, thereby reducing the risk of photodamage to neighboring healthy organs and tissues [410,411]. Besides, it was found that the photothermal conversion agents are mainly from nanomaterials constituting nanozymes, whereas some photosensitizers such as ICG and TMB are also used [412–414]. In MHT, AMF and magnetic nanoparticles are two vital factors for heat production. Magnetic nanoparticles generate heat via relaxation and hysteresis losses upon exposure to an alternating magnetic field [415]. In hysteresis loss, thermal energy arises from the collision and friction among magnetic domains aligning with the external alternating magnetic field [416]. In relaxation loss, encompassing Néel or Brown mechanisms, single-domain superparamagnetic nanoparticles produce heat by surmounting the energy barrier linked to rotational motion in an alternating magnetic field [417]. Moreover, nanozymes and hyperthermia therapy have synergistic effects. On one hand, nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms can act as photothermal conversion agents or magnetic nanoparticles. On the other hand, heat can further enhance the catalytic activities of nanozymes. Besides, the robust light absorption and photothermal conversion capabilities, or unique magnetic properties of many nanozyme-based platforms, originally developed for PTT or MHT, render them equally useful in cancer detection and diagnostic procedures (e.g., PAI for PTT, MRI for MHT) [161, 206, 418–423].

In 2023, Zhu et al. designed PtSn bimetallic nanoclusters (PtSn BNCs) with CAT- and POD-like activities for tumor PTT combined with catalytic therapy [161]. Initially, it was established that Pt50Sn50-PEG BNCs exhibited the highest absorption capacity for 808 nm laser and displayed superior photothermal performance in terms of photothermal conversion. The aqueous solution of Pt50Sn50-PEG displayed a concentration-dependent temperature increase under 808 nm laser irradiation, with a 200 μg mL−1 concentration experiencing a rapid temperature elevation of 24.9 ℃ (
Fig. 19Bi), which was further proved sufficient to kill tumor cells (Fig. 19Biii). Moreover, the stability of photothermal performance of the Pt50Sn50-PEG BNCs was proved after the four heating/cooling cycles (Fig. 19Bii). Besides, the POD- and CAT-like activities of Pt50Sn50-PEG were enhanced under 808 nm laser irradiation. In vitro studies also found that Pt50Sn50-PEG plus laser irradiation exhibited good antitumor activity. Among four groups including control, laser irradiation, Pt50Sn50-PEG and Pt50Sn50-PEG plus laser irradiation groups, Pt50Sn50-PEG plus laser irradiation treatment represented the strongest lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release and caspase-3 expression in 4T1 cells (Fig. 19Biv, Bv). In vivo studies further verified the strongest antitumor activity and tumor inhibition ability of Pt50Sn50-PEG plus laser irradiation in 4T1-bearing mice among four groups (Fig. 19Bvi, Bvii). An in vivo biosafety and toxicity of this treatment approach was also proved. Besides, Pt50Sn50-PEG BNCs was found to possess abilities of CT and PAI both in vitro and in vivo. A notable distinction in CT signal within tumor tissues was evident following a 6-hour intratumoral injection (Fig. 19Bix). Conversely, the most robust PA signal intensity within tumor tissues was achieved after a 6-hour intravenous injection (Fig. 19Bx). These results demonstrated that theranostics of Pt50Sn50-PEG BNCs, can not only achieve mild photothermal-enhanced catalytic therapy with strong antitumor effects but also achieve real-time PA and CT dual-modal imaging. Moreover, this study serves as a paradigm for the synergistic benefits of combining nanozyme-based catalytic and photothermal therapies in cancer treatment.

Besides PTT, PDT is another important light-dependent treatment approach. PDT was not only a clustering keyword but also the highest-frequency keyword associated with cancer treatment in the scientometric analysis, indicating its wide application in this field. PDT comprises three essential components: a photosensitizer, a precise wavelength of light, and the presence of oxygen. And there are two types of PDT (type-I and type-II), depending on their mechanisms of threptic effects. In the process of type-I PDT, during light irradiation, photosensitizers in their ground singlet state (S0) can be excited to the singlet state (S1) and subsequently undergo intersystem crossing to transition to the triplet state (T1). In this triplet state (T1), the photosensitizers can participate in hydrogen abstraction or electron transfer reactions with cellular substrates, resulting in the generation of free radicals [123,435,436]. And these free radicals will react with surrounding H2O or O2 to generate ROS, such as H2O2, •OH, or O2 •-. In the process of type-II PDT, photosensitizers at triplet state (T1) will directly transfer energy to nearby triplet oxygen (3O2, ground state of O2) for generating cytotoxic 1O2 [123,437]. Besides, two types of PDT can occur simultaneously, and the ratio of type-I/type-II as well as their efficacy depends on properties of laser irradiation and photosensitizers, and O2 level. Compared with type-I PDT, type-II PDT has a greater demand for oxygen supply. Since type-II PDT is the current mainstream and used in most studies related to cancer therapy, increasing oxygen supply around tumor sites is regarded as a common and useful approach for more effective PDT [123,289].

Table 11 summarized recent applications of nanozyme-based catalytic therapy plus PDT. With the development of nanozymes and catalytic medicine, nanozyme-based nanoplatforms with CAT (or SOD)-like activity as well as photosensitizer-loading ability are greatly popular in this field. This can be indicated from Table 11, in which nanozymes with CAT (or SOD)-like activity hold great majority. Photosensitizers can be integrated or conjugate or loaded into nanozymes to form nanozyme-based nanoplatforms. And the commonly used photosensitizers include chlorin e6 (Ce6), aggregation-induced emission (AIE) molecules, ICG, HPPH, etc. In the aspect of treatment approaches, Catalytic therapy plus PDT, Catalytic therapy plus PDT and CDT, and Catalytic therapy plus PDT and PTT are popular combination regimens. Besides, similar to PTT, many other nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms also possess imaging abilities [158, 202, 204, 438–441].

In a recent study, Duan et al. [158] proposed a Schottky junction nanozyme, CoPc-Mn/Ti3C2Tx, for cancer theranostics, involving multimodal PDT/PTT/CDT guided by PAI ( Fig. 20A). To improve water solubility, biocompatibility, and tumor-targeting capabilities, the study incorporated PEG and the AS1411 aptamer. Significantly, the presence of the Schottky junction in the structure promoted efficient electron-hole separation and exhibited a notable redox potential. This facilitated the autonomous generation of H2O2 and O2within the system. Initially, the CoPc-Mn/Ti3C2T x nanozyme was employed to mediate type I PDT, resulting in the production of •O2 - through electron transfer when exposed to NIR 808 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 20Bii). Then, the generated •O2 - can be converted to H2O2 due to the SOD-like activity of CoPc-Mn/Ti3C2T x nanozyme upon NIR light irradiation. And H2O2 can react with •O2 - to further generate •OH and O2 •- Therefore, Mn/Ti3C2T x nanozyme can achieve self-supply of H2O2 and O2, with the record of changes for H2O2 concentration and dissolved O2 (Fig. 20Bi, Biii). Based on the self-supplying H2O2 and O2, CoPc-Mn/Ti3C2T x nanozyme can further mediate CDT (Fig. 20Biv-vi) and oxygen-dependent type II PDT (Fig. 20Bvii-ix) with stronger efficiency. Both in vitro and in vivo studies verified the strongest antitumor effect of synergistic PDT/PTT/CDT induced by CoPc-Mn/Ti3C2T x under NIR light irradiation. And in vivo biocompatibility evaluation also showed a good biocompatibility and a negligible influence on the central nervous system of CoPc-Mn/Ti3C2T x -PEG/AS1411. Additionally, the study obtained time-dependent photoacoustic (PA) images and assessed the signal intensity in B16-tumor-bearing mice following the administration of CoPc-Mn/Ti3C2T x -PEG/AS1411 at different time intervals. These results underscore the role of this nanoplatform in guiding PDT/PTT/CDT through photoacoustic imaging. Overall, this work integrated four kinds of therapies, as well as PAI into one nanoplatform with self-supplying ability of H2O2 and O2, which achieved excellent synergistic therapeutic effect. Moreover, this work also sets an example for laser-based nanozyme-based theranostic nanoplatform.

SDT represents a novel non-invasive approach to cancer treatment. As introduced, light-dependent therapies will inevitably encounter the difficulties of deep tissue penetration, determining that PTT/PDT is more beneficial and advantageous in the field of superficial tumors [451]. Expanding from PDT, SDT has emerged as a promising approach, offering the advantage of enhanced tissue penetration compared to PTT and PDT [452]. The constituent of SDT is similar to PDT, including an ultrasound at specific frequency and intensity, a sonosensitizer, and oxygen [453]. SDT operates through two primary mechanisms: ultrasonic cavitation and ROS generation. Ultrasonic cavitation is initiated within a sound field and involves two distinguishable forms, namely, inertial and non-inertial cavitation [454]. Inertial cavitation can inflict tissue damage through elevated temperature and pressure [455]. Conversely, non-inertial cavitation triggers antitumor effects through bubble oscillations, heat production, microflow, and localized shear stress [456,457]. Moreover, ultrasonic cavitation can further activate sonosensitizers, promoting the generation of ROS. For ROS generation, ultrasound- and cavitation-excited sonosensitizers are thought to be the core element. Activated sonosensitizers can directly interact with dissolved oxygen or other substrate molecules, resulting in the generation of free radicals. Moreover, they can release and transfer the energy back to the oxygen to produce 1O2 [458]. Besides, pyrolytic effects and sonoluminescence may also take part in this process and also generate ROS [459]. Considering the insufficiency of current research on SDT, more studies are needed to clarify the detailed mechanism of SDT.

Currently, research on SDT mainly focused on two aspects, including finding excellent sonosensitizers and improving the hypoxic TME. Since nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms can not only act as or deliver sonosensitizers but also enhance oxygen supply and ROS increase, they have been considered advantageous in the field of SDT (Table 12). The traditional sonosensitizers mainly include hematoporphyrin or porphyrin derivatives, Ce6, IR780, TiO2, MnO2, etc. [451,454]. Owing to the distinctive physical and chemical attributes, nanomaterials have become a significant source of sonosensitizers [453]. In most studies of nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms for SDT, nanomaterials themselves act as the sonosensitizers (Table 12). Similar to PDT, CAT-like activity is also a key catalysis activity for this type of nanozymes. Besides, these nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms can also act as theranostic platform, which is similar to PDT and PTT [61,460,461]. Table 12 also indicated that Catalytic therapy plus SDT combined with other types of therapies is probably a popular trend.

In 2023, Cai et al. introduced a nanozyme-based piezoelectric sonosensitizer, consisting of Hf-based MOF UIO-66 and Au NPs (UIO-66-Au NPs) [462]. Under ultrasound (US) irradiation, UIO-66 generates an inherent electric field, facilitating the separation of electrons and holes, resulting in the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS). The incorporation of Au NPs enhances electron transfer, reducing electron-hole pair recombination and enhancing the piezoelectric properties of UIO-66. UIO-66-Au NPs demonstrated increased efficiency in generating ROS (1O2 and •OH) compared to UIO-66, as shown in Fig. 21Bi, Biii (1O2) and Fig. 21Biv (•OH). Fig. 21Bv elucidated the enhancement mechanism of the piezoelectric SDT effect. Additionally, it was noted that the Au NPs exhibited significantly improved POD- and CAT-like activities due to the piezoelectric field generated under US irradiation. Upon incubation with H2O2 (using TMB as an indicator), it was observed that the UIO-66-Au NPs combined with US irradiation group exhibited higher absorption intensity compared to both the UIO-66-Au NPs and UIO-66 groups with US irradiation (mediated through SDT-generated •OH). In contrast, the single US and single UIO-66 groups displayed no catalytic activity (Fig. 21Bvi). Michaelis-Menten kinetic analysis confirmed that US irradiation enhances the peroxidase-like activity of UIO-66-Au NPs (Fig. 21Bvii). The dissolved oxygen content in H2O2 solution with UIO-66-Au NPs increased, particularly with US introduction, indicating improved catalytic activity due to the enhanced piezoelectric effect by the built-in electric field (Fig. 21Bviii). In vitro results showed that UIO-66-Au NPs combined with US irradiation induced significant cytotoxicity (Figure 23Ciii), while UIO-66-Au NPs or UIO-66 without US irradiation did not. In vivo, UIO-66-Au NPs/US demonstrated the most substantial inhibition of tumor growth among the tested groups (Fig. 21Cii). Importantly, there were no significant changes in the mice's body weight, confirming the safety of UIO-66-Au NPs (Fig. 21Ci). Overall, this work provides a typical example of catalytic therapy plus SDT, in which a built-in electric field in a piezoelectric MOFs-based nanozyme was activated by ultrasound, and thus enhancing the ROS production efficiency for SDT and the POD- and CAT-like activities for catalytic therapy. Meanwhile, the CAT-like activity also improves oxygen supply in the TME and promotes the efficiency of SDT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Current challenges and limitations of nanozymes for cancer research
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to natural enzymes, nanozymes possess unique catalytic advantages such as high activity, mild reaction conditions, good stability, low cost, and ease of large-scale production. Additionally, nanozymes exhibit physicochemical properties inherent to nanomaterials (e.g., optical, acoustic, electrical, magnetic), which have led to their continuous development and application in cancer diagnosis and treatment in recent years. They demonstrate enhancements and organic integration with existing technological methods and means. Alongside the escalating interest in nanozyme research, several challenges emerge. In the realm of nanozyme research, these challenges include catalytic activity, specificity, targeting ability, effect of TME on nanozymes’ functions, biocompatibility and biosafety.

Enhancing catalytic activity involves developing single catalytic activity, combining multiple catalytic activities, and the need for improving catalytic activities, switching, and even precise control of catalytic reaction progression. Regulating specificity entails controlling catalytic reaction specificity, substrate specificity, site-specificity, and stereoselectivity. Targeting specificity includes addressing organ and tissue targeting, targeting cancer lesions, and specific targeting within target cell types or even organelles. The influence of TME on nanozyme function, such as the impact of low pH, hypoxia, poor light penetration in cancer environments, and the masking of catalytic or binding sites by complex substances within the body, also plays a crucial role.

Biocompatibility and biosafety considerations are equally important. To address the limitations and potential biosafety issues concerning current nanozymes, various approaches can be pursued. On one hand, safer and more diverse chemical and biological source materials can be sought for nanozyme synthesis and production. This involves analyzing the structure-function relationship of nanozymes through experimental design and deep learning, and employing rational design methods such as structure optimization, size control, shape modulation, and cascade modification to meet specific catalytic, targeting, metabolic, and biological functional needs. On the other hand, research should focus on the catalytic mechanism of nanozymes, catalytic processes, subcellular localization, and the underlying mechanisms of interaction factors and regulatory pathways in vivo, as well as understanding the roles of these processes in clinical cancer diagnosis and treatment. Additionally, suitable in vivo detection, tracking, and application methods for nanozymes should be developed, along with appropriate scenarios for nanozyme applications.

Lastly, key technological breakthroughs in the complex and challenging synthesis of nanozymes and the refinement of large-scale production routes are necessary to meet the production demands in practical applications. Simultaneously, establishing quality control and functional evaluation systems for nanozymes is essential for fostering the sound development of the field and industry, paving the way for clinical translation.

In the realm of cancer diagnosis and treatment, nanozymes also face some unique challenges. In terms of diagnosis, it is imperative to develop and assess nanozyme detection targets reliably. This process encompasses identifying dependable cancer biomarkers and evaluating their relevance and representativeness for diseases. The precision of these evaluations significantly influences the diagnostic reliability of nanozymes. Furthermore, enhancing the diagnostic efficiency of nanozymes involves optimizing reaction efficiency, operational versatility, and result readability. Simultaneously, it is crucial to contemplate the suitability of nanozymes for current detection methods and instruments, while also exploring the creation of new nanozyme detection techniques.

In cancer treatment, the diversity of cancer types, tissue organ localization, and cell types, as well as the complexity of carcinogenesis, regulatory pathways, and regulatory targets, pose challenges to the practical application of nanozymes in cancer treatment.

The main challenges in utilizing nanozymes for cancer treatment include the impact of TME on catalytic activities of nanozymes, such as pH, hypoxia, and changes in light exposure. Additionally, the formation of protein coronas on nanozyme surfaces may shield catalytic sites, substrate binding sites, or hinder product release. The influence of tumor parenchyma and complex structures on delivery and targeting poses difficulties in delivering nanozymes to solid tumors or reaching target cells deep within the tumor. Moreover, these structures may affect the uptake or metabolism of target cells. Unclear diagnosis at different stages of tumor development and clinical features can affect the design of catalytic activity and the timing window for using nanozymes, thus impacting treatment efficacy. Cancer cell resistance to treatment drugs also presents a significant challenge in achieving effective cancer treatment, along with the challenges in combining nanozymes with other cancer treatment drugs and modalities.

It can be observed that many of the challenges faced by nanozymes in cancer diagnosis and treatment are also present in other diagnostic and therapeutic processes. These challenges depend on the continuous in-depth research and understanding of the occurrence and development patterns of cancer. However, the unique material advantages and functional strengths of nanozymes, involving multidisciplinary and interdisciplinary intersections in materials, structures, life sciences, medicine, and computing, even extending to the development of integrated diagnosis and treatment platforms and delivery platforms, are unparalleled. We believe that nanozymes hold tremendous potential for development and application in cancer diagnosis and treatment.

During the past decade, nanozymes have gained significant attention for cancer detection/treatment. Prior to the year 2017, the main focus of nanozymes was on cancer detection. After the year 2018, nanozymes are increasingly applied in cancer treatment and have become one of the hottest topics in the nanozyme field. Moreover, the multifunctional nanozyme platform with cancer detection and treatment is probably the future trend of this field. For cancer detection, both in vitro and in vivo nanozyme-based approaches have yielded considerable data. With the development of signal generators for in vitro detections such as FIA, CLIA, ECIA, ECLIA, PECIA, nanozyme-based cancer detection approaches with abilities of signal amplification and enhanced specificity have been studied. Since the identification of a series of biomarkers can increase the accuracy and sensitivity, nanozyme-based multi-detection approach such as multi-analyte immunoassays and immunosensing arrays are becoming a promising field. For in vivo cancer detection applications, there are more challenges and requirements for nanozyme-based detection approaches. Therefore, constructing diagnostic and therapeutic nanozymes with tumor imaging and treatment capabilities or using imaging to guide tumor treatment or monitor treatment effects is a current research hotspot. Nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms with excellent multimode imaging ability are probably more popular since they can not only adapt to different conditions but also improve the observation of tumors. For cancer treatment strategies catalytic activity-based strategies such as ROS increase, GSH depletion, oxygen supply, starvation therapy, and TME remodeling and ICD, and nanostructure-based strategies such as drug delivery are potential contenders. Based on these treatment strategies, we made the investigations and statistics of recent related studies of this field. It was found that nanozyme-based combined or synergistic therapies are the mainstream. Nevertheless, the ultimate goal of combined therapies is not simply an adding process, instead, an intrinsic enhancement mechanism of two or more treatment approaches should be discovered. We further categorized the cancer treatment approaches of nanozymes into catalytic therapy induced/plus CDT, catalytic therapy induced/plus immunotherapy, catalytic therapy plus chemotherapy, catalytic therapy plus radiotherapy, catalytic therapy plus PTT or MHT, catalytic therapy plus PDT, and catalytic therapy plus SDT, with the summaries of recent relevant studies and necessary discussion of typical work. Most studies did not specifically consider tumor types, instead, they prefer to validate the feasibility of nanozymes or nanozyme-based nanoplatforms. However, different tumors may have different TMEs or location that are suitable for different therapeutic strategies and treatment approaches. For example, superficial skin tumors such as melanoma are more suitable for light-dependent therapy, but deep tumors are not. Since different tumors may express certain specific targets, it will be essential to modify nanozymes to target these molecules in future studies.
Supporting Information is available from the corresponding authors.

This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 82273479 to L.Z.; U23A20686 and L2324225 to K.F.), Research Fund of Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Department (Grant No. 20240305079YY to L.Z), China-Japan Union Hospital and College of Basic Medical Sciences, Jilin University Union Project (Grant No. KYXZ2022JC05 to L.Z.), and Maryland Department of Health's Cigarette Restitution Fund Program (CH-649-CRF to DVK).
